title: The Environmental History of Subsistence and Extraction in Africa before 1900
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: How did Africa become the poorest continent? To answer this, this course explores relationships between environment and society from ancient times through the beginning of the colonial era. Topics include foraging, cultivating, herding, metallurgy, population movement, long-distance trade, the Atlantic slave trade, European conquest, and imperial occupation. We also examine concepts of wealth, poverty, and justice. Reviewing case studies from different regions, we consider the ecological, demographic, social, economic, and political factors that shaped production, as well as the global developments that made Africa vulnerable to resource extraction for external profit. No experience of African history required. P

title: The Modern Chinese Nation: An Idea and Its Limits
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: How did the Chinese empire become a nation-state? This course introduces the history of Chinese societies from 1850, when a massive civil war destabilized the country and sent a new wave of migrants across the globe. We will explore how local and regional society was affected by the spread of new conceptions of the Chinese nation, new kinds of government, and cultural and technological innovations. Coursework addresses the construction of race, ethnicity, and religion as well as gender and sexuality; the effects of war and catastrophe; different formulations of revolution and self-strengthening; and more.

title: China's Socialist Dreams
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: A history of the People's Republic of China under Mao in its context. We’ll explore the appeal of anarchism and socialism in the early 20th century and experiments in rural utopianism and worker autonomy. To understand the Chinese Communist Party, we’ll examine it as upstart, revolutionary vanguard, strategic ally, army, and state builder. Using new research on 1950s-70s Chinese society, we’ll ask how the Maoist party-state reached the lives of urbanites and farmers; affected gender relations and constructions of sexuality, age, and family; and shifted formulations of ethnicity, race and China’s place in the world.

title: Postwar Japan
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course is for students interested in exploring Japan’s remarkable cultural, political and social transformations from the closing days of the Second World War, through its emergence as an apparent exemplar of democratization’s potential and capitalism’s benefits, and on to the contemporary era. Lectures, readings and films will explore the legacies of the war and the Occupation, the so-called “economic miracle” (and its effects on the environment), the protest movements of the 1960s and beyond, and Japan’s complicated relationships with its neighbors, with the U.S., and with its own recent history. Open to all students.

title: Formation of the Classical Heritage: Greeks, Romans, Jews, Christians, and Muslims
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Explores essential social, cultural, and religious foundation blocks of Western Civilization, 200 BCE to 800 CE. The main theme is the eternal struggle between universalism and particularism, including: Greek elitism vs. humanism; Roman imperialism vs. inclusion; Jewish assimilation vs. orthodoxy; Christian fellowship vs. exclusion, and Islamic transcendence vs. imminence. We will study how ancient Western individuals and societies confronted oppression and/or dramatic change and developed intellectual and spiritual strategies still in use today. Students should be prepared to examine religious thought from a secular point of view. There is no prerequisite or assumed knowledge of the period. P

title: The Viking Age
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: For two centuries, Viking marauders struck terror into hearts of European Christians. Feared as raiders, Norsemen were also traders and explorers who maintained a network of connections stretching from North America to Baghdad and who developed a complex civilization that was deeply concerned with power and its abuses, the role of law in society, and the corrosive power of violence. This class examines the tensions and transformations within Norse society between AD 750 and 1100 and how people living in the Viking world sought to devise solutions to the challenges that confronted them as their world expanded and changed. P

title: Cultural History of the Netherlands in a Golden Age and a Global Age
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Between 1580 and 1690 two nations emerged in Europe from what had been one unified region. To the north, the Dutch Republic gained its independence from Spain and developed as a bastion of liberty, ideas in ferment, visuals arts, Calvinist faith, science, technology, global economic reach. To the south, the "loyal" Netherlands, now Belgium, returned to the Spanish and Catholic fold, but sustained its leading position in the arts, competed in global trade, and negotiated a new compromise of government. In this course we present an interdisciplinary, comparative view of the "two" Netherlands and their legacy in the world. P

title: The Global British Empire, 1600-The Present
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course charts the long history and continuing legacies of the British Empire, an entity that has transformed every single continent over the last four centuries and is widely associated with the makings of the modern world. We examine how and why a powerful and expansive British Empire emerged and sustained itself. Equally, we zoom in on the regular contestation and even outright rebellion that this transcontinental polity inspired. This course is an opportunity to think connectively and comparatively about historical experiences in America, India, the Caribbean, and Africa among multiple other British imperial spaces. We will track the making and unmaking of the British Empire by studying the evolution of global trade, labor regimes including slavery, the consumption of commodities such as sugar, as well as new ideas about governance, race, and identity.

title: The French Revolution
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course aims to provide a basic factual knowledge of the French Revolution, an understanding of the major historiographic debates about the revolutionary period, and a sense of the worldwide impact of events occurring in late-eighteenth century France. A strong historiographic focus will direct our attention to the gendered nature of the revolutionary project; the tension between liberty and equality that runs throughout French history; the intersection of race and citizenship in the Revolution; and the plausibility of competing social, political, and cultural interpretations of the Revolution.

title: Paris: Sacred and Profane, Imagined and Real
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Paris has been called the capital of modernity, the capital of the nineteenth century, and the capital of the black Atlantic. This course explores how Paris grew from a small settlement into a vast city with an enormous global impact. Covering the settlement of the Celtic Parisii in the mid-third century BCE through the present, the course investigates the dynamic relationship between urban space, public activism, racism, and colonialism. It also considers who has been excluded from the city’s complex mythology and how these myths impacted experiences of the “other” (including people of color, low-income people, Jewish people, and women).

title: The Mexican Revolution
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: To study the Mexican Revolution is to examine the sweeping history of Modern Mexico: from the Liberal reforms of Benito Juárez to the enduring power of the Partido Revolucionario Institucional (PRI); from peasant revolutionary Emiliano Zapata to his namesake Zapatistas of Chiapas; from Pancho Villa’s mass revolutionary army to transnational mystic Teresita Urrea; from the landlord Francisco Madero who led the insurgency to Lázaro Cárdenas who enacted land and labor reforms; from the constant flows of migrants crossing the border back and forth to Mexico’s defiance against Trump’s wall.

title: History of the Andes
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Before the Spanish invaded in the 1530s, western South America was the scene of the largest state the New World had ever known, Tawantinsuyu, the Inca empire. During almost 300 years of colonial rule, the Andean provinces were shared by the "Republic of Spaniards" and the "Republic of Indians" - two separate societies, one dominating and exploiting the other. Today the region remains in many ways colonial, as Quechua- and Aymara-speaking villagers face a Spanish-speaking state, as well as an ever-more-integrated world market, the pressures of neoliberal reform from international banks, and the melting of the Andean glaciers.

title: Amazonia from the Prehuman to the Present
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course merging lecture and discussion will examine the fascinating and contested history of the largest rainforest on the planet and one of the world’s most complex fluvial ecosystems: Amazonia, in equatorial South America, from its pre-human history to the present day. The course will include readings and discussions on the region’s ecological origins; the social history of its diverse Indigenous populations, immigrant groups, and African-descended populations; exploration myths and European colonial projects; and more recent efforts to exploit and protect Amazonia’s extraordinary natural and human resources. The course will use tools and resources from archaeology, anthropology, biology, and social and cultural history, and will also examine popular representations of the Amazon through novels, newspapers, podcasts, and film.

title: Latin American History and Film: Memory, Narrative and Nation
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course is centered around the relationship between history and storytelling. It takes as a starting point that history is itself political, and asks that we always consider both how and why we tell stories about the past. Together, we will critically examine a broad range of films and texts that engage with the Latin American past, and explore what they have to say about how gender and sexuality, imperialism, slavery, the church, revolution and repression shaped the region. How do films represent the past, and how do we approach “historical film”? How have narrative and documentary films been used as in various social and political struggles? Each week, we will examine documentary, narrative, or experimental films in relation to other modes of historical storytelling, from academic historiography to graphic novels, first person testimonios, memoirs, oral histories, and journalism.

title: Bankrupt: An Economic and Financial History of the Middle East from the 19th - 21st Centuries
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course critically examines the economic, and particularly, the financial history of the modern Middle East in the 19th and 20th centuries. It is structured chronologically and thematically. It starts in the mid-19th century by examining the role of European states, as well as private European investment firms and oil companies, in facilitating the colonization of the region via loans, capitulations, and extractive concession agreements. It then shifts to the post-colonial period, studying how western oil companies, banks, investment firms, and multi-national organizations shaped the trajectories of the newly-independent states in the Middle East.

title: The American Revolution
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course will explore the period of the American Revolution from the 1760s through the turn of the nineteenth century. Taking a broad view of the conflict and its consequences, we will situate the American colonies in their North American and Atlantic context, examine the material and ideological concerns that prompted the Revolutionary War, and trace the consequences of the conflict for the nation that followed. Students will be invited to look beyond the Founders to the experiences of women, slaves, Native Americans, common soldiers, and Loyalists.

title: Making America Modern
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course surveys a crucial period in American history between the end of Reconstruction and the beginning of World War I. During this time, the United States transitioned from a relatively fragmented, traditional, and largely agricultural society into one that was remarkably diverse, increasingly urban, and highly industrialized. In surveying this important transitional period, we will pay particular attention to far-reaching changes in the nation's business and economic life, its social movements, as well as its cultural developments, all with an eye to understanding how the United States became one of the world's most commanding economic, political, and cultural powers.

title: First Nations: The People and Cultures of Native North America to 1800
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course explores the history of North America through the eyes of the original inhabitants from pre-contact times up through 1800. Far from a simplistic story of European conquest, the histories of Euroamericans and Natives were and continue to be intertwined in surprising ways. Although disease, conquest, and death are all part of this history, this course also tell another story: the big and small ways in which these First Nations shaped their own destiny, controlled resources, utilized local court systems, and drew on millennia-old rituals and practices to sustain their communities despite the crushing weight of colonialism. P

title: American Slavery
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This lecture course will address the history of slavery in America. We will trace the emergence of slavery in the New World, with a heavy emphasis on slavery in the U.S. South, and a focus on the relationship of slavery to the emergence of systems of racial and gendered power. The course is broad in scope, beginning with the emergence of the slave trade and concluding with a look forward to the ways that the history of slavery continues to impact the way race and gender (as well as sexuality and class) structure our lives today.

title: Cities and Inequality Since 1920: The United States
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This lecture course takes up the relationship between cities and social and economic inequality—especially racial, but also other forms of social disadvantage along lines of class, gender, sexuality, indigeneity, and geography. Its focus is the urban history of American cities, suburbs, and towns and how urban development since the advent of the motor-car suburb in the 1920s shaped American social life. Students will learn about and explore topics such as zoning, racial redlining and other forms of segregation, real estate markets, the home building industry, urban public policy and law, urban leisure, transportation, and many others. At the center of the course is the question of how people live in dense connectivity and community and what the history of the modern American city can tell us about the nature and durability of social inequality.

title: Empires in America to 1890
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: In this class, we’ll consider some of the forms of empire-building by various groups of indigenous and colonizing peoples in what is now the United States in order to understand the development of imperial U.S. power in both domestic and international contexts. Rather than resting upon a foregone conclusion of European settler colonial “success,” the course explores the contingent and incomplete nature of empire-building even within unbalanced power relationships.

title: The Intellectual History of Black Women
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course will introduce students to the intellectual productions and theoretical traditions of African American women. Focused on the canonical texts of African American women, this class gestures toward diaspora as well. Moving chronologically from the history of slavery to the present will require that we simultaneously confront the question of what counts as “intellectual” history. Thus even as we will read the written words of black feminists across time, we will also call into question what Barbara Christian calls “the race for theory,” turning also to resistance practices, material culture, and bodily performance as sites of black feminist theorization.

title: Resisting Empire: Gandhi and the Making of Modern South Asia
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Gandhi's India tracks the emergence and transformations of British colonial rule in the Indian subcontinent, the insurgencies and the cultural and economic critiques that shaped anti-colonial nationalism, the conflicts that fueled religious differences and the ideas that shaped non-violent civil disobedience as a unique form of resistance. With readings from Gandhi, Marx and Tagore, amongst others, this course interrogates relationships between power and knowledge, histories from below, as well as violence and political mobilizations that would, by the mid-twentieth century, bring down an empire and create a bloody and enduring divide with the birth of two nation-states.

title: Environmental History of Commodities: Past and Present Worlds
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: We are surrounded by commodities today: from teas and sugars on our breakfast tables, oil and gases running our cars to metals powering our phones and laptops. Have you wondered where these commodities came from? How does a seemingly ‘remote’, ‘war-torn’ frontier get connected to global financial centers? What happens to forests, rivers, hills, mountains, and Indigenous ecologies after a ‘resource’ is identified? This course studies the environmental histories of commodity production and consumption from the early modern to present times. It focuses on the afterlives of commodities—the unintended effects of subjecting vibrant life worlds to colonial-capitalist logics. Prior background in history/environmental studies is helpful but not necessary.

title: From Medieval Bedlam to Prozac Nation: Intimate Histories of Psychiatry and Self
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Humankind has long sought out keepers of its secrets and interpreters of its dreams: seers, priests, and, finally, psychiatrists. This lecture course will introduce students to the history of psychiatry in Europe, the United States, and beyond, from its pre-modern antecedents through the present day. Our focus will be on the long age of asylum psychiatry, but we will also consider the medical and social histories that intersect with, but are not contained by, asylum psychiatry: the rise of modern diagnostic systems, psychoanalysis, sexuality and stigma, race, eugenics, and pharmaceutical presents and futures.

title: Unearthing the Body: History, Archaeology, and Biology at the End of Antiquity
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: How was the physical human body imagined, understood, and treated in life and death in the late ancient Mediterranean world? Drawing on evidence from written sources, artistic representations, and archaeological excavations, this class will explore this question by interweaving thematic lectures and student analysis of topics including disease and medicine, famine, asceticism, personal adornment and ideals of beauty, suffering, slavery, and the boundaries between the visible world and the afterlife, in order to understand and interpret the experiences of women, men, and children who lived as individuals—and not just as abstractions—at the end of antiquity. P

title: Roman History I: The Rise and Fall of an Imperial Republic (CLAS 1310)
description: Interested students must register for CLAS 1310.

title: Women, Gender and Feminism in Early Modern Italy (ITAL 1262)
description: Interested students must register for ITAL 1262.

title: The Persian Empire and Achaemenid Culture (CLAS 1230)
description: Interested students must register for CLAS 1230.

title: The Civil Rights Movement (AFRI 1090A)
description: Interested students must register for AFRI 1090A.

title: Legal History: The Case of the Middle East
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This seminar is devoted to exploring questions of law and legal history in the Middle East. Together we shall navigate a range of imperial, colonial, and national legal systems in the region via select case studies from early modern to contemporary times. Major themes include not only Islamicate legal traditions and jurisdictions, but the intersections, tensions, and synergy between Islamic law and Roman, Persianate, and European imperial legacies; fellow Abrahamic faiths and the interplay between religion and secularism; and the evolving role of international law including human rights and humanitarian law. From early schools of Islamic jurisprudence to Ottoman, Egyptian, and Iranian constitutions, and the role of lawyers and judiciaries from decolonization to the Arab Spring uprisings, our goal is to explore the depth, diversity, and transformation of legal cultures and institutions across different epochs of Middle East history.

title: The Indigenous Worlds of the American Revolution
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course considers the wider area of the American Revolution through the experiences and actions of Indigenous nations. Although the usual story of the American War of Independence is centered on the political standoff between the American colonists and the colonies (and eventual states), the view from Indian country looked vastly different, with divergent allegiances, and experiences ranging from 1760 to 1795, during which the American Revolution was only one conflict.

title: Thinking Historically: A History of History Writing
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Philosopher George Santayana famously warned that “Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.” Ten years later, industrialist Henry Ford perhaps even more famously dismissed that notion: “History is more or less bunk.” What we mean by history and how we construct and use it are essential questions in all societies. Thinking Historically explores how we view and employ the past. The course examines major ways of interpreting the past through a survey of historians and methods and studies how history is produced, used, and misused, by professionals as well as by the public.

title: Combating Colonialism: Comics and Indigenous Storytelling
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course would begin with decolonial theory, history of indigenous storytelling, then move into the history of comics and highlight indigenous stereotypes, and then move into how indigenous people have used this very white-American form of storytelling and reclaimed indigenous portrayals.

title: On the Move: Problems and Histories of Tourism and Mobilities
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course examines histories of modern recreational tourism and the politics of mobility more generally. Tourism is a fascinating point of departure through which we can examine how histories of empire, consumerism, gender, and the environment all converge upon practices many (but certainly not all) of us take for granted. The premise of this seminar is based on two basic questions: through tourism, how do we change a place and how does that place change us? How does human mobility produce a new set of economic, social, and political relations? The answers will vary across time and space, but through engagement with scholarship and various primary sources we can come to better understand how the pleasure modern-day travelers derive from temporarily being someplace else is ultimately something deeply embedded into the structures and historical processes of the modern world itself.

title: Indigeneity and Assimilation in Southern Africa: The Khoe-San
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: South Africa is infamous for apartheid, the starkest form of settler colonialism. Yet 20th-century segregation reversed centuries of assimilationist policies against South Africa’s First Peoples: the Khoe-San. From the mid-17th century, this distinctive cultural group endured environmental and epidemiological shocks, conquest, and genocide. Their descendants only survived within a Dutch-speaking underclass. This seminar traces their survivance: from early encounters and violent subjugation to claims-making against 19th century British liberalism, ending with recent assertions of indigeneity among “Coloured” descendants in decolonizing South Africa. Khoe-San history—trauma, adaptation, and endurance—forms a crucial chapter in African history and comparative colonial history. P

title: More-Than-Human Histories of Africa
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This seminar offers a more-than-human consideration of Africa’s past. It takes physical matter, microbes, plants, animals, the built environment, and people all as historical actors. Our premise will be that agency is not a human preserve but is embedded in and shared across heterogenous assemblages. We sample recent writings in Africa’s environmental history while considering more-than-human implications of Africa’s position as the cradle of humanity, the source for the Atlantic slave trade, the imperial possession of Europe, and an underdeveloped margin of the global capitalist economy.

title: Medicine and Public Health in Africa
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course explores the major debates in the history of medicine in Africa during the nineteenth and twentieth centuries and highlights the coexistence of a variety of healing traditions and medical understandings across the continent. It will focus on the following questions: What are some of the ways Africans practice and understand medicine? How have these practices interacted with other medical systems? What impact did colonialism have on the production of medical knowledge? How were practices and treatments evaluated and deemed effective? By whom and on what grounds? And how have independent African states addressed these critical issues?

title: North African History: 1800 to Present
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course focuses on the francophone Maghrib (Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia) and offers an introduction to major themes in the history of Africa and the Arab world in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Students will gain the tools to analyze and historicize the dynamic history of this region. We will examine a range of topics, including the transformations of pre-colonial social, economic and cultural patterns, conquest and resistance, comparative histories of colonialism, nationalism, decolonization and revolution, the consolidation of postcolonial states, regional cooperation, the rise of Islamism and civil conflicts, and the Arab Spring.

title: Heaven Above, Suzhou and Hangzhou Below: Urban Culture in Early Modern China
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The commercial boom of sixteenth and seventeenth century China stimulated the growth of a lively popular culture in the great cities of the southeast—Nanjing, Suzhou, and Hangzhou. These cities became magnets for ambitious scholars, pleasure-loving merchants, courtesans, artists, and writers and sites for the production of some of the great masterpieces of Chinese vernacular fiction, drama, book art, and painting. After some background reading in socioeconomic history, the course focuses on analysis of the literature and art of the period and what it reveals about the short-lived “floating world” of late imperial China. P

title: Southeast Asian Food History
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This new course aims to introduce students to Southeast Asian history through the lens of food. Spanning the region, it will examine the economic, cultural, medicinal, and social life of food while considering, too, how food--as method and archive--opens new horizons for the study of Southeast Asian history.

title: The Enchanted World: Magic, Angels, and Demons in Early Modern Europe
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: European fascination with the unseen world reached its highpoint alongside the Renaissance, Reformation, Scientific Revolution,and Enlightenment. Between 1500 and 1800, theologians, natural philosopher, princes, and peasants devoted enormous energy to understanding, communicating with, and eliminating a host of ethereal creatures, including ghosts, angels, demons, vampires, nature spirits, and witches. Some also sought to access the praeternatural powers that these creatures seemed to command. This course explores the intellectual, social, political, and religious origins of the interest in this unseen world, the structures Europeans created to grapple with it, as well as the factors that ultimately led to its demise.

title: Women in Early Modern England
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Selected topics in the social history of early modern England (c.1500-1800), with particular emphasis on the experiences of women. Themes to be addressed will include the family, working life, education, crime, politics, religion, and the early feminists. Not open to freshmen sophomores. P

title: Hannah Arendt and Her World
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Hannah Arendt (Hanover, Germany 1906 – New York 1975) is increasingly recognized as a pivotal thinker about her world and ours, bridging Europe and America, philosophy and politics, while taking on such key issues as migration, statelessness, the refugee experience; nationalism, totalitarianism, and Zionism; the politics of race and anti-Semitism; the relation between thinking and acting. In this seminar we will read some of Arendt’s key works, including “We Refugees,” “Zionism Reconsidered,” The Origins of Totalitarianism, and Eichmann in Jerusalem, accompanied by other texts that inspired her or were in turn inspired by her, from Max Weber to Judith Butler.

title: Making Revolutionary Cuba, 1959-Present
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: In January 1959, the forces of rebel leader Fidel Castro entered Havana and forever altered the destiny of their nation and world. This seminar will consider the six decades that followed the rise of a new revolutionary order, as well as its global repercussions. We will examine how political hegemony was achieved as well as the silences and exclusions on which it rested, with particular attention to ideology, race, sexuality, and culture.

title: Approaches to the Middle East
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: When and why did the Middle East emerge as a field of study? What are the different approaches that frame our understanding of this pivotal region, and how did they change over time? This upper-level seminar explores these questions within the larger context of colonial and national and encounters that have shaped modern regimes of knowledge production. The class features visits by leading scholars from different disciplines who reflect on the questions they ask and how they go about answering them. Readings range from canonical works to innovative new scholarship. No pre-requisites but previous coursework on this region recommended.

title: America and the Middle East: Histories of Connection and Exchange
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This seminar explores the history of connection and exchange between the diverse peoples and societies of two constructed regions: the Middle East and North America. Proceeding chronologically, we begin with the global context of Columbus’s fateful voyage in 1492, before turning to colonial Spanish, Portuguese, and English-British intersections with the Ottoman Empire and Morocco. While including Latin American, Caribbean, and Canadian case studies, the bulk of our course explores US ties to the “Mideast/MENA” region during the 19 th -21 st centuries. Yet, this isn’t a conventional course in diplomatic history. Rather, we’ll read closely for underlying socioeconomic, cultural, and intellectual processes—trade, migration, education, and evolving conceptions of race, religion, and citizenship—themes often overlooked by foreign relations, watershed events, or conflict. Our goal: to recognize how US-Middle East ties are far more complex, rich, and deep-rooted than is generally assumed.

title: Israel-Palestine: Lands and Peoples I
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The Holocaust largely destroyed the centuries-long Jewish civilization in Europe. For Zionism, originating in late nineteenth-century antisemitism and East-Central European ethno-territorial nationalism, the “final solution” proved the need for a Jewish-majority state in Palestine. Yet the majority of the population in Eretz Israel was Palestinian. The creation of Israel in 1948 was the outcome of a bitter war with the local Arab population and the surrounding states, in the course of which most of the Palestinians were expelled or fled, facilitating the establishment of a Jewish-majority state. The seminar will discuss the fraught question of the two traumatic events of the Holocaust and the Nakba (the expulsion of 1948), and propose that we can both better understand these events, and begin the long path to reconciliation, by applying the tools of empathetic first-person history.

title: Early American and Atlantic Money
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The history of finance has become a crucial site for studying governance and statecraft, for recovering the organizing logic of capitalism, and for recognizing the structures of power in any given society. Topics include the recurring debates over metallic and paper currencies, the emergence of a national banking systems, and the technologies of coinage, assaying, and counterfeiting. Particular focus on the relationship among finance, colonization, and slavery, as well as the many speculative monetary systems that circled around the Atlantic world from the fifteenth century through the nineteenth century.

title: American Legal History, 1760-1920
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Upper-level undergraduate seminar examining the history of issues that have been contested in recent U.S. legal decisions and practices. The main period covered is 1780-1920, but we will also consider the use and misuse of the history of this period in legal decisions and debates of the past two decades. Among the topics covered are Native American sovereignty; firearms use and regulation; slavery, servitude, and emancipation; women’s rights and reproductive freedom; and the meaning of citizenship. Enrollment limited. To be considered for admission, students must contact the instructor before the beginning of the semester. Instructor permission required.

title: Lesbian Memoir
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This capstone seminar explores the genre of lesbian memoir through lenses both historical and theoretical. We will think together whether memoir constitutes a primary source for historical study, and its place in lesbian history. We will consider feminist theoretical questions posed by this genre of writing: What (or who, or when) is a lesbian? Is the category of the lesbian rooted in gender essentialism or disruption? How (or when, or if) did the category of lesbian become marked as white?

title: US Empire and Archives
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: It will be a capstone seminar for the History Department that gives students hands-on experience with documents archived at Special Collections in the Hay Library. The theme of US Empire is expansive, relating to peoples, places, and events within the metropole (i.e., the United States) and the larger world in the mid-20th century to the present. Focus will be on hegemonic and counter-hegemonic ideologies and actions, especially concerning racial capitalism, settler colonialism, free trade imperialism, Third Worldism, gender and queer politics, ethnicity, and more. Archival collections include the Hall-Hoag Collection, the Mumia Abu-Jamal Collection, and others.

title: Modern South Asia and the Image Archive
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This seminar will look at how historians, art historians and artists have used an array of visual materials in the archive to make arguments about the past. We will read texts on the history of photography, on popular ephemera, from film studies, as well as interrogate the work of artists who draw on archives. How do we "read" images, interpret them, and how have they impacted our understanding of the past?

title: Environmental Pressures of South Asia
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: South Asia is one of the most densely populated geographical regions of the world. The traditional environmentalist view of separating/protecting ‘nature’ from human settlements often leads to conflicts of resources/land use here. This course studies the evolution of environmental governance in the diverse ecologies of South Asia across four substantive and methodological parts. Part I involves readings on environmental and agrarian systems in the Mughal Empire from the precolonial period (1526-1757) in South Asia. Part II involves readings on forestry laws from the British colonial period (1757-1947) in South Asia. Part III involves reviewing a series of books and films on environmental history in Asia. Part IV involves researching case studies focusing on local environment cultures from the postcolonial period (1947 to present) in South Asia. The course requires writing a final research paper based on a case study.

title: The Intellectual History of Imperialism
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: What is an empire? What does an empire do? What is the difference between imperialism and colonialism? How have historical actors as well as historians between the seventeenth and twentieth centuries answered these questions? This seminar considers the long history of intellectual debate over imperialism and its relationship with state formation, capitalism, labor, subjecthood, and the environment. We will read proponents of imperial expansion, advocates of imperial reform, and fierce critics of imperialism. Readings will include canonical texts authored by major historical figures such as John Locke and Vladimir Lenin as well as pamphlets and legislative debates that document the everyday practice of imperialism. In engaging with such primary sources, we will consider how rival visions of imperialism shaped ideological traditions as diverse as liberalism, fascism, conservatism, and communism.

title: Race, Incarceration, and Freedom in the Americas
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course examines the history of mass incarceration in the Americas, focusing on the relationship between race, policing, and punishment from colonial Latin America to twenty-first century United States. Together, we will explore ways of thinking about the long history of punishment and its relationship to racial and gender politics as well as different forms of labor exploitation, and think about how different temporal and geographic framings help us think in new ways about the history of incarceration and (perhaps) point to new futures. Among the topics we will explore are the ways contemporary carceral regimes have been shaped by the afterlives of slavery; the shifting meanings of rehabilitation; the experiences of incarcerated individuals; the Cold War politics and transnational roots of militarized policing in the United States; and the ways gender, sexuality, and age shape imprisonment across the hemisphere.

title: Modern Environmental Movements from Conservation to the Rights of Nature
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: What are the politics of creating and maintaining a livable Earth after industrialization? How has the environment figured in movements for equality and justice? This senior seminar examines environmental issues and responses under capitalism in North America and socialism in Russia and Eastern Europe. The course covers conservation and preservation; Indigenous anticolonial movements; responses to chemical and plastic pollution; anti-nuclear protest; environmental justice; climate change and anti-fossil fuel movements; and new forms of environmental organizing including the Rights of Nature movement and degrowth. Focused on the 19th and 20th centuries, the class will situate movements in ecological, political, and economic context, assess outcomes, and examine the relationships between economic form and politics. Using primary and secondary sources in a range of media, seminars will include short lectures, discussions, and student-led case studies that build toward a substantive final research project.

title: Histories of the Future
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course is for students interested in how ideas about what the future of human societies would look like have developed over time, and in the impact of those ideas on cultural, social and political norms. We will look carefully at examples of early modern prophecy before turning to the more recent emergence of theories of economic and social progress, plans for utopian communities, and markedly less optimistic and often dark visions of where we’re headed. We will also explore the roles capitalism, popular culture, and science have played in shaping the practices and vocabularies associated with imagining the future.

title: Planning the Family
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course explores 20th-century efforts to address population growth and family planning in a variety of global contexts, ranging from macro-level initiatives to individual practices. For some women, state leaders, and international experts, contraception meant sexual liberation, increased autonomy, and the reduction of economic hardship. But for others, the same biomedical technologies were associated with colonial control and efforts to reduce “undesirable” people. How is it possible for contraception to hold such expansive and contradictory meanings? In order to assess this central question, students will engage with critical theories of gender, race, class, nationalism, and decolonization.

title: The History of Childhood
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Childhood is commonly assumed to be natural, universal, and unchanging. Children everywhere have certain things in common: dependence on adults in their early years, developmental immaturity, and pent-up creativity and resilience. But the lived experience and the cultural understanding of childhood are re-invented daily, and differently, across both time and geography. This course explores the long-term history of childhood in different regions of the world, through readings in history, anthropology, psychology, educational theory, and literature.

title: Climates of Inequality: The Plantation-to-Petrochemical Complex
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: What is the relationship between land, labor, and global capitalist modernity? How does studying environmental injustice help resituate America’s colonial past and its imperial present? This interdisciplinary course introduces students to one of the most pressing environmental justice struggles in the nation today: Cancer Alley, USA. Together, we will explore the entwined histories of slavery, settler colonialism, and racial capitalism in Louisiana and the broader Gulf South, connecting colonial history to ongoing forms of state violence. Readings draw heavily from Environmental History, Native American and Indigenous Studies (NAIS), and Black Studies to address themes of ecological devastation and embodied violence, while also highlighting practices of resistance and resurgence from historical uprisings to contemporary cultural revitalization efforts. Students will learn to critically analyze how racialized systems of power and privilege structure inequality while envisioning alternative, more just futures.

title: Worlds on the Move: Migration and Exchange Across Europe and the Mediterranean, 1492–1700
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: How did people travel across the Mediterranean in the early modern period? What documents and economic resources enabled their mobility? How did cities respond to migration, and what did it mean to cross borders in a world of empires, republics, and religious frontiers? This course explores the mechanisms and experiences of movement, examining how migration shaped the cultural, social, and political development of early modern societies. Drawing on the instructor’s expertise, the course places special emphasis on female mobility and the influence of gender relations on migratory experiences. Students will engage with various primary sources, such as archival documents, maps, iconographic materials, and literary texts, which will provide them with essential tools and methods for practicing history and deepen their understanding of how historians work.

title: History of Healing, Medicine, Technology, and Global Health in Africa
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: History of Healing, Medicine, Technology, and Global Health in Africa will examine the political, social, and cultural dimensions of health and healing across the African continent. Through historical case studies and contemporary debates, students will explore how indigenous knowledge, medical technologies, and public health interventions have shaped, and have been shaped by, Africans, colonialism, nationalist movements, and global health agendas. Students will be required to participate in a workshop and engage with primary and secondary sources to analyze the intersections of medicine, science, and technology and global health. Students will participate in seminar discussions and expert-led sessions, develop a research project that critically examines a historical or contemporary issue in African health and healing, and produce written reflections, reports, and oral presentations that connect historical insights to present-day challenges. The course emphasizes interdisciplinary thinking, historical analysis, and persuasive communication skills.

title: Work: A Global History
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: What is work and who is a worker? The industrial male wage worker stubbornly persists in historical and cultural imaginaries of what work under capitalism looks like. Yet we know that most work – and most people who work – have not fit into this narrow conception. This course examines the history of work and workers in a predominantly capitalist world economy from roughly the early nineteenth century until the late twentieth century. Taking a global perspective with a particular focus on geographies in the Global South, topics will include studies of enslavement, agrarian labor, coerced labor, informal labor, migrant labor, wage-work, labor under state socialism, reproductive labor, and the relationship between workers and political movements. We will begin with various theoretical considerations of work and labor under capitalism and then move into a roughly chronological narrative.

description: Guided reading on selected topics. Section numbers vary by instructor. Please check Banner for the correct section number and CRN to use when registering for this course.

title: History Honors Workshop for Prospective Thesis Writers
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 05 or 06. Enrollment limited to students in the History (AB) program. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Prospective honors students are encouraged to enroll in HIST 1992 during semesters 5 or 6. HIST 1992 offers a consideration of historical methodology and techniques of writing and research with the goal of preparing to write a senior thesis in history. The course helps students refine research skills, define a project, and prepare a thesis prospectus, which is required for admission to honors. Students who complete honors may count HIST 1992 as a concentration requirement. Limited to juniors who qualify for the honors program.

title: History Honors Workshop for Thesis Writers, Part I
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 05, 06, 07, 08, 09, 10, 11, 12 or 13. Enrollment limited to students in the History (AB) program. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: All students admitted to the History Honors Program must enroll in HIST 1993 for two semesters of thesis research and writing. They may enroll in the course during semesters 6 and 7, or 7 and 8. Course work entails researching, organizing, writing a history honors thesis. Presentation of work and critique of peers' work required. Limited to seniors and juniors who have been admitted to History Honors Program. HIST 1993 is a mandatory S/NC course. See History Concentration Honors Requirements.

title: History Honors Workshop for Thesis Writers, Part II
prerequisites: Prerequisites: HIST 1993 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This is the second half of a year-long course, upon completion the grade will revert to HIST 1993. Prerequisite: HIST 1993.

title: Preliminary Examination Preparation
description: For graduate students who have met the tuition requirement and are paying the registration fee to continue active enrollment while preparing for a preliminary examination.

title: Reading and Research
description: Section numbers vary by instructor. Please see check Banner for the correct section number and CRN to use when registering for this course.

title: History Now
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Focused on current trends in historical writing and research, with emphasis on presenting a diverse range of cutting-edge work in historical and historiographical context. Required for all first-year PhD students in History.

title: The Roots of History
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: “The Roots of History” encourages critical thinking about some of the different ways in which historians approach thinking and writing about the past. In particular, we will explore some of the major theoretical stances that have influenced the discipline of history. Our focus throughout will be the interplay between theory and practice. By examining how historians have grappled with questions posed by influential thinkers (often working within other fields of knowledge), we will chart the trajectory of the discipline and assess its working methods. Required for all first-year PhD students in History.

title: Writing History
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Required of all 3rd semester Ph.D. students.

title: The Practice of History
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students in the Ancient History (PHD), History (AM) or History (PHD) programs. Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This graduate seminar is about the professional skills, tools, and careers of historians—from hallmark best practices to new directions. We will engage different kinds of careers historians are pursuing today, the practical skills and resources necessary to meet them, and how historians are deploying those skills in multiple professional arenas, both in and beyond the academy. We shall also discuss some of the prevalent (although often unwritten) social and cultural norms of academia and university life to prepare you to navigate your training, career, and various institutional contexts that accompany and follow completion of a Ph.D. in History

title: Prospectus Development Seminar
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This collaborative seminar focuses on identifying and contextualizing a dissertation topic; choosing a dissertation committee and a support network; designing and executing plans for research and writing; and articulating a thesis project as a prospectus, grant proposal, and other oral and written forms. This is a required course for and open only to third-year students in the History Ph.D. program.

title: Rethinking the Civil Rights Movement
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This graduate course encourages a rethinking of the complex components, arguments and activities that have characterized what we have come to know as the Civil Rights Movement, concentrating primarily on African American agency, actions and politics, through careful reading of recent scholarship in the field. While knowledge of U.S. history is preferred, this course asks larger thematic questions about protest movements (the role of the state, relationships with and between oppressed groups and organizations, and periodization), that will interest non-Americanists also. Some of the topics covered include: gender, organizing and strategies, the local, global ramifications and interactions, organizational structures and politics, and the recent concept of the Long Civil Rights Movement. M

title: Graduate Readings in Early American History
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: No description available.

title: Latin American Historiography
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course examines the development of historical writings on Latin America produced in the United States from the late nineteenth century until the present. We will focus on themes, such as race, gender, labor, subaltern studies, dependency theory, postcolonial analysis, and post-modernism, to understand the diverse approaches to Latin American history. M

title: Anticolonial Lineages: Readings from South Asian History
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This reading course examines scholarship from South Asian history on colonial modernity, formations of religious communities, subaltern consciousness, anticolonial thought, caste, gender and sexuality, amongst other themes. We will consider debates in the field, theory from the south, and questions of historical method. It is open to both graduate students as well as advanced undergraduates.

title: First Person History in Times of Crisis: Witnessing, Memory, Fiction
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This seminar examines the relationship between History as a narrative of events and history as individual experience. Postulating that historical events as related by historians were experienced in numerous different ways by their protagonists, the seminar focuses on the complementary and contradictory aspects of this often fraught relationship at times of crisis, especially in war and genocide. While much time will be spent on World War II and the Holocaust, the seminar will engage with other modern wars and genocides across the world. Materials will include eyewitness reports, postwar testimonies and trial records, memoirs and relevant works of fiction. Open to graduate students only. M

title: Seascapes of History
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This seminar explores the recent “oceanic turn” in history, examining how and why the sea and the maritime matter to interpretations of the past. Key readings will include general works that theorize new maritime history and thalassography, and studies focused on the history of specific oceanic and maritime areas (e.g. the Atlantic, the Pacific, the Indian Ocean, the Caribbean, the Mediterranean), which illuminate sub-themes such as migration, colonization, empire building, trade, sailors’ culture, piracy, cultural attitudes toward the sea, religion and sea, and maritime environmental history. Readings will be drawn from a wide range of chronologies as well as geographies.

title: Orientalism
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Europe’s fascination with “the Orient” generated a major tradition of intellectual, literary, and artistic production, beginning in the medieval era and persisting to the present day. This seminar explores the social and intellectual history of Orientalism, considering the ways that it has intersected with the study of power, race, sexuality, religion, and language. Readings to include Edward Said’s seminal work on this subject alongside several critiques, correctives, and debates that have emerged in recent years.

title: Perspectives on Jewish History
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Jewish history has a complex dynamic. The development of Jewish society is fed not only by Jewish tradition but also by developments in non-Jewish society. Equally, the place of Jews in the non-Jewish world is determined not only by the attitudes of non-Jews but also by the economic, social, and political agency wielded by Jews themselves. This graduate seminar will, in a broad chronological framework, examine the complicated interplay of forces which shaped both the Jewish experience in the non-Jewish world and the non-Jewish world’s treatment of its Jewish minority. Among the topics to be discussed: the places of Jews in premodern Christian and Muslim societies; Jewish early modernity; the historical trajectory of Jew-hate; changing Jewish responses to non-Jewish culture; Jewish power, powerlessness, and political development; the modern Jewish experience before, during, and after the Holocaust.

title: Thesis Preparation
description: For graduate students who have met the residency requirement and are continuing research on a full time basis.

title: History Dissertation Writing Workshop
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This seminar is for History PhD students in residence at Brown who are post-exams and are at any stage of writing their dissertations. It is intended to support dissertators by providing a structured community for setting and sharing goals, writing, and discussing chapters-in-progress. It meets every other week and is a half credit S/NC course.

title: The Chisolm Massacre: Reconstruction and the Politics of Violence
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This humanities seminar takes a broad and varied look at Reconstruction, the United States’ first great experiment in racial justice. It situates this post–Civil War effort to remake the former Confederacy and integrate the formerly enslaved as American citizens, and the white supremacist backlash to it, by exploring broader discussions of American political and racial violence and the political history of the Reconstruction era (1863–1877) and its aftermath. The seminar foregrounds case studies of Mississippi communities riven by Ku Klux Klan terrorism, race riots, and vigilante murders, especially an incident called “the Chisolm Massacre” (1877). The course also examines the memory of Reconstruction and its portrayal in popular culture, from D.W. Griffith’s film Birth of a Nation (1915) through Steven Spielberg’s Lincoln (2012), for example.

title: Home and Away: South Asian Literature in the Global Diaspora (COLT 0711U)
description: Interested students must register for COLT 0711U.

title: From Turtle Island to Abya Yala: Art of the Indigenous Americas (HIAA 0076)

title: Environment, Violence, Repair
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 03 or 04. Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Recent conversations in environmental justice foreground harm and repair, as the scope of environmental questions expands to include exposure, toxicity, war, and infrastructures. This seminar positions violence and repair as entry-points to an ethnographic understanding of environmental harm, disturbance, and change through an interdisciplinary body of work that includes environmental history and film. The course connects recent ethnographic accounts of exposure, extraction, and pollution to critiques of violence, imperialism, and statecraft, and asks about the possibilities and limitations of repair, refusal, and survival. Reading critical theory on violence next to environmental degradation opens up space to think and learn about how colonial and state projects terra/aqua-form the environment and lifeways and calls us to think critically about environmental struggles from the perspective of resistance, repair, and non-western and non-secular responses to disaster, disruption, and change.

title: The Cogut Institute for the Humanities Research Seminar
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 07, 08, 09, 10, 11, 12 or 13. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This yearlong seminar involves reading and discussing in-progress research by the annual fellows of the Cogut Institute for the Humanities, an interdisciplinary group of faculty, postdoctoral fellows, graduate students, and undergraduates engaged in extended research on a major project or honors thesis. Students read a wide range of works-in-progress, prepare questions and participate in seminar discussions, serve as first questioners for specific sessions assigned to them in advance, and present their own work twice during the year. Students are required to complete both semesters (HMAN 1000A in the fall and HMAN 1000B in the spring) to receive credit. Admission to the course requires that students have received the Cogut Institute Undergraduate Fellowship for the year in which they enroll. More information about the fellowship and application process may be found at https://humanities.brown.edu/apply/undergraduate-fellowship.

title: The Cogut Institute for the Humanities Research Seminar
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 07, 08, 09, 10, 11, 12 or 13. Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This yearlong seminar involves reading and discussing in-progress research by the annual fellows of the Cogut Institute for the Humanities, an interdisciplinary group of faculty, postdoctoral fellows, graduate students, and undergraduates engaged in extended research on a major project or honors thesis. Students read a wide range of works-in-progress, prepare questions and participate in seminar discussions, serve as first questioners for specific sessions assigned to them in advance, and present their own work twice during the year. Students are required to complete both semesters (HMAN 1000A in the fall and HMAN 1000B in the spring) to receive credit. Admission to the course requires that students have received the Cogut Institute Undergraduate Fellowship for the year in which they enroll. More information about the fellowship and application process may be found at https://humanities.brown.edu/apply/undergraduate-fellowship.

title: Decolonizing Data: Approaches from Caribbean Digital Humanities and Beyond
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: What does it mean to decolonize methods of data collection, construction, analysis, ownership, and sharing in the digital humanities? To what extent is decolonization possible within existing institutional frameworks and digital technologies? This course will approach humanities “data” broadly as quantitative as well as archival, textual, and cultural, and explore innovative approaches to decolonizing data in Caribbean digital humanities projects and critical DH. Through hands-on workshops, theoretical readings, and evaluation of projects such as the Early Caribbean Digital Archive and Algorithmic Justice League, the course will interrogate questions of data ethics in the colonial archive, data justice, digital practices of care, and community-centered archiving. Students will find or create a dataset and develop a data management, preservation, and sharing plan that puts into practice decolonizing methodologies. No prerequesites or experience with coding, data, or digital technology required.

title: Transnational Feminist Knowledge Production & Practices: Perspectives from East & West Asia-EAST1706
description: Interested students must register for EAST 1706.

title: The Politics of Symbolic Worth: Groups, Identities, and Status in American Politics
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: What leads politics to become meaningful, engaging, and contentious for members of the public? In this course, we will examine how American politics is shaped by struggles between social groups over the meanings and status of group identities. First, we will examine theoretical perspectives suggesting political behavior can be seen as symbolic action to affirm—and sometimes legally enforce—one’s own morality, social status, lifestyle practices, and vision of the proper social world. Then, we will apply this framework to understanding two areas of American politics: the politics of white racial resentment, and stalled political action to address climate change. This course will provide conceptual tools for understanding what’s at stake in public debates for many Americans, and how elite-public interactions over the meanings and status of group identities have shaped American politics in the 20th and 21st centuries.

title: Foreign Policy Worlds: Diplomatic Designs and International Relations
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Foreign policy unfolds through carefully crafted encounters and exchanges in both domestic and diplomatic arenas. International actors leverage narratives, venues, dress, gestures, images, and soundscapes to sustain relations with others, pursue security goals, and affect the world they live in. At the intersection of the humanities and social sciences, this course engages participants in new research on White House state dinners while drawing from a range of historical and global case studies from the early modern era to the present. Diplomatic dinners provide a lens into the everyday practices, protocol-driven scenes, mediatized moments, and spectacular occasions that are constitutive of the international stage and the theater of trust, respect, competition, conflict, and hegemony. At the end of the semester, a planning memo outlining the purpose and components of a state visit offers an opportunity to put knowledge into practice.

title: We the People
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: How do individual people come together to make a People? We will read key ancient texts including Aristotle, Livy, and Cicero, enlightenment thinkers like Rousseau and Hobbes, and modern theorists from Arendt and Schmitt to Chakrabarty and Mouffe to see how the “we” of We the People has been used both to include and exclude. We will also consider practices of political action both ancient and modern. Can a People create itself by a vote, a manifesto, or a riot? What is the relationship between the People and populism? The course is explicitly grounded in the ancient world; as well as analyzing how ancient concepts have echoed through the centuries, we will also look to the distant past for difference, asking how we might propose alternative genealogies of the People.

title: Settler Colonial Cities: Indigenous Histories, Presents, and Futures
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The course examines the Indigenous histories, presents, and futures of settler colonial cities. Founded on Indigenous lands expropriated for town building, modern cities in settler colonial societies were and are complicit in processes displacing Indigenous people and appropriating their cultures, objects, and ancestors. We will draw on anthropology, archaeology, archival history, geography, heritage preservation, public art, and urban studies to explore colonialist tactics of spatial removal and symbolic and documentary violence aimed at erasure. Most importantly, we will look at how Indigenous people have challenged claims of their absence in urbanizing landscapes through their oral histories, writing, and material culture, and by their presence and diverse experiences in cities in the United States, Canada, Australia, and beyond.

title: Hospitality and Literature
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course will explore ways of thinking about literature in relation to the concept of hospitality, and vice-versa: we’ll test the proposition that literary hospitality helps us conceive of a hospitality irreducible to subjectivity, property, or power. Texts to be examined include the Bible, The Odyssey, and other classical texts; philosophical writing by Immanuel Kant, Emmanuel Levinas, and Jacques Derrida; and Franz Kafka’s The Castle, Clarice Lispector’s The Passion According to G.H., and poems by Paul Celan.

title: Archival Activism and Digital Worlds
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course examines the possibilities and perils of “archival activism”—the concerted effort to preserve the stories of marginalized people as part of the historical record—in the digital age. The growth of social media and the ubiquity of digital devices that can record, photograph, and upload events from a first-person perspective has made all participants in historical events into potential archivists. Through an examination of digital documentation created by ordinary citizens during major events in recent history—including the COVID-19 pandemic, racial justice protests, and the January 6 insurrection—we ask, what kinds of archives are we and our digital devices creating? What kinds of histories can or should be written with these records? And what roles can, and should, social scientists and other scholars of the contemporary be playing in collecting and preserving digital histories of the now?

title: Critical Race Theory Now
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Moving beyond the reductive and polarizing deployment of Critical Race Theory (CRT) in current political discourse, this interdisciplinary seminar traces its history as a legal intervention foregrounding race and racism in the development of U.S. law and in the (infra)structures of U.S. civic, socioeconomic, and political life. Together, we will explore the central tenets of CRT, including the ubiquity and permanence of racism, intersectionality, whiteness as property and currency, and redistributive justice. Reading seminal texts of CRT alongside contemporary African American literature, we will attend to specific topics, such as affirmative action, immigration, mass incarceration, and reparations. The overarching inquiry of this course is: (1) how might the law be complicit in upholding racial hierarchy as well hierarchies of gender, class, sexuality, and citizenship, and (2) how might the law and literature be used to dismantle those same hierarchies?

title: The Politics of Health and Disease (POLS 1295)

title: Combating Colonialism: Comics and Indigenous Storytelling (HIST 1958D)

title: Artificial Images (GRMN 1702)
description: Interested students must register for GRMN 1702.

title: The Black Outdoors
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: What are the racialized and gendered politics of the outdoors? How does the concept of the Black outdoors challenge dominant narratives of nature, freedom, and belonging? What forms of Black cultural production have emerged from social life lived underground, under siege, and in flight? And how might Black artistic or cultural practices help us rethink what it means to be outside? This course explores the multiple dimensions of the Black outdoors — as theory, practice, poetics, and survival. We will examine how Black life has been shaped by, and has shaped, the outdoors and the environment more broadly — from the fields of the plantation to the vastness of outer space, from the breathless garrett to the open breeze, from the underwater afterlives of the Middle Passage to the speculative geographies of Black futures.

title: Wet Ethnographies
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Recent experimental ethnographies explore the relationship between research and writing, textual strategies, and the politics of ethnography. Experimental methods provide tools to think and write about projects of coloniality and capital and how they shape the material environment. This seminar introduces students to ethnographies that take place in environments where the boundaries between water and land get blurred, in bodies, deltas, river embankments, swamps, mangrove wetlands, and reclamation areas that carry signs of both enabling and challenging former and continuing colonial projects of empire or capital. We will critically read and examine ethnographic works that tackle “wetness” and “fluidity” as theoretical tools to understand temporality, circulation, and flow and challenge linear development projects from a feminist perspective. The course fosters attunement to more-than-human life and materialities of soil, water, and land/seascape through ethnographic practices of walking and drawing, among others.

title: Philosophical Foundations of Cognitive Science (PHIL 1890K)
description: Interested students must register for PHIL 1890K.

title: Toxic: Health, Environment, and Life after Harm (ANTH 1314)
description: Interested students must register for ANTH 1314.

title: Bottom Ecologies: Blackness, Queerness, and the Environment
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: How have Black queer communities — and minoritarian communities more broadly — transformed environments of shame and disposability into sources of erotic power, pleasure, and collective survival? Building on Toni Morrison’s “Sula” and theories from Black feminisms, queer-of-color critique, and Black ecological thought, this course views the “bottom” as Black queer ecological thought. We will trace how Black queer communities generate counter-knowledge, reshape racialized and sexual subjectivities, and challenge conventional environmental discourse by queering ecological analysis. Ultimately, this course examines the “bottom” as both a sexual position and an ecological site historically raced, and marked as disposable, dirty, abject. Readings from Morrison, Jennifer Nash, Audre Lorde, Darieck Scott, Juana María Rodríguez, Kathryn Bond Stockton, and Leo Bersani will guide our exploration of how landscapes of racial abjection and erotic desire converge, offering new critical tools for environmental humanities.

title: Environmental Anthropology (ANTH 1555)
