description: A novel without the letter "E", 100,000-billion sonnets by permutation and texts that take the shape of a Mobius-Strip-- all this time and more, as workshop participants try their hands in writing in response to problems created by and inspired by a group of writers engaged in strange constraints and procedures. Instructor permission required. S/NC.

title: Script to Screen: Fundamentals of Filmmaking
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course explores ways in which a series of short filmmaking exercises can serve both first-time filmmakers, providing a solid foundation, and more experienced ones, providing low-stakes avenues for creative experimentation. Students who have some proficiency in screenwriting or poetry are especially encouraged to apply. The course aims to open writers up to the possibility of making films, while also investigating how production experiences can inform future writing projects. Assignments involve a variety of filmmaking techniques and tools to investigate visual language and its interplay with sound (with and without dialogue), and can be tailored to students with all levels of experience.

title: Script to Screen: Scene Work
prerequisites: Prerequisites: LITR 0110E , 0210E , 1010E , 1110U or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Enrollment limited to students in the Literary Arts (AB) program. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This intensive script development and directing workshop is designed for students who have some proficiency in screenwriting, and little to no directing experience. Basic shooting and editing skills are helpful, or will be learned in the first half of the semester. The course aims to serve as a two-way bridge, opening screenwriting students up to the possibility of directing their work, while also investigating how acting and directing experiences, and workshopping material with actors, can inform future screenwriting. Activities include learning acting and directing techniques, scene analysis, writing and revising scenes, casting and working with actors, creating a scene through improvisation, and filming practice versions of scenes. Guests will join us for workshops and final reviews.

title: Generative Poetry Workshop
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This intensive generative poetry workshop combines in-class workshops, reading and review assignments, craft talks, in-class writing exercises and course portfolios/packets. The in-class workshops will focus on work brought in for discussion and review and will also include in class writing workshops responding to prompts. The course is designed to encourage the generation of new poetry by students and to develop good reading practices to engender good poetry-writing craft.

title: Verse Africa: A Critical Reading & Writing Workshop
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: In this course we will critically engage the poetry and poetics of contemporary African, African American and Caribbean poets to examine the incredibly diverse aesthetic trends as well as their intersections. Students will also produce both critical and creative responses to the texts we read and will have the opportunity to regularly workshop their own poems influenced by the works we engage.

title: Why YA?
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: “All American fiction is young adult fiction,” claims cultural critic A.O. Scott. Is he right? Does it matter? In this course, we’ll undertake a survey of American young adult literature as we attempt to answer a few central questions: Can anyone actually define what “young adult” literature is? What do writers hope to accomplish through the use of young protagonists? Who gets to be “young”? And how are our cultural beliefs about “youth” – and its comrades “time” and “mortality” – expressed through these works? To explore these questions, we’ll read works by Jamaica Kincaid, Sandra Cisneros, Neal Shusterman and Akwaeke Ezewki, among others. We’ll also attempt our own experiments with some of the aesthetic modes and thematic questions that have come to define the genre.

title: Writers-in-the-Community Training & Residencies
prerequisites: Prerequisites: LITR 1010A , 1010B or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course will operate mostly “in the field.” We will spend some weeks discussing pedagogical approaches to teaching creative writing in community settings. We will thereafter train in residence, observing a poetry residency at a local elementary school, with visits to other community settings as well (sites to be determined). We will continue to discuss pedagogy, classroom practices and management, administrator-writer relations, and all other necessary logistical planning throughout the semester. By week 7, students will engage in their own writing residencies in pairs or small teams, working in a community setting of their choosing (K-12 school, shelter, library, etc.).

title: Ecopoetics in Action
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: What we have perpetrated on our environment has certainly affected a poet's means and material, thinking and life. Can poetry embody values that acknowledge the economy of interrelationship between human and non-human realms? How might poetry register the complex interdependency that draws us into a dialogue with the world, moving beyond theme and reference? In this class, we will think-with ecoscapes, ecotones, and Animalia (ourselves included, along with our humanimal contexts), and other life and nonlife forms, via the lapping possibilities of poetic language. We will write, read, discuss, create, think, experience. Do be prepared for some “field work”.

title: Memory’s Imagination
prerequisites: Prerequisites: LITR 0100A , 0110A , 0210A , 1010A , 1110Z , 1150M , 1151Q , 1152V , 1152Y , 1153A , 1153B , 1153C , 1153D , 2010A or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Taking inspiration from the autobiographical work of newly minted Nobel Laureate, Annie Ernaux, we will examine and produce works of fiction, creative narrative non-fiction and hybrid texts that cleave, in that good old double-sense, to personal experience and/or personal passions. Works by Marcel Proust, Virginia Woolf, James Baldwin, Michael Ondaatje, John Keene, Jessica Au and Svetlana Alexeivich will be on the menu to inform and inspire. Students can expect a substantial weekly reading load and should come to each class prepared to discuss the assigned texts and linked creative assignments.

title: Curatorial Considerations: Writing Magical Realism
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This semester, we’ll give your forays into the unusual the airtime and love they deserve, and nurture these evolutions toward the realms of absurdism, fabulism and magical realism. We will discuss the alteration of realism as a way of putting the narrative at hand to its highest use. We’ll also examine music and artwork that pushes the boundaries of the “real” in terms of form. While not a 1:1 correlation in terms of the departures we make in literature, they’re worth considering as interdisciplinary paradigms that push the envelope. We’ll discuss the music/artwork briefly at the beginning of each course session. You need not write anything formal about these pieces–just note how they defy expectation to affect your understanding.

title: Creative Process Workshop
prerequisites: Prerequisites: LITR 0110H , 0210H , 1010H , 1110N , 1150B , 1151X , 1152E , 1152Y , 1153H , 2010A , 2010B , 2110O or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Creative Writing can be understood as a method of turning ideas, sounds, images, snippets, fragments, dreams, research -- and any and all of life's materials -- into fully realized literary works. The "work" of literary works is hidden when we read books and published authors, and thus our sense of how unique and wondrous work is achieved can be underdeveloped or perfectionistic. In other words, when we rely on only a reader's perspective to guide us as artists, it provides little to no sense of artistic path. This workshop is for writers of all levels to explore several works-in-progress in more depth. The result will be an enhanced relationship to the works (and to process) and to a lifelong and sustainable creativity. Becoming a more curious and helpful reader of others is another tangible and valuable outcome. Open to all.

title: Conjuring God
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Is there a place for capital-g God in contemporary fiction? If so, how might the concept of God help us explore our individual and collective search for meaning? And what might narratives about encounters with the divine tell us about the idiosyncrasies of belief (in God, in ideas, in the self)? As we read works by Albert Camus, Graham Greene, Shusaku Endo, Marilynn Robinson and Ursula Le Guin, among others, we’ll discuss why writers still attend to notions of the sacred and divine decades after “the death of God.”

title: Reading and Making with Large Language Models
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Large Language Models can be used to generate text with a facility that some of us hail as ‘human-level.’ Informed by theory – chiefly linguistic and language philosophical – this workshop seminar allows its participants to explore what we mean when we say that artificial intelligence reads and writes; and what is implied for our own processes of literary or language-art making when we agree or intend to read and write with (and/or against) such systems. Apart from its reading-informed discussion, the workshop will provide time and critique for its participants’ projects, which will engage and experiment with the seminar-generated ideas and actual machine-learned techniques.

title: Against Mastery
prerequisites: Prerequisites: LITR 0100B , 0110B , 0210B , 1010B , 1110X , 1110Y , 1152C , 1152N or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: In “A Black Poetics: Against Mastery” Dawn Lundy Martin argues against mastery as a creative, compositional mode. She writes that mastery “is an attempt to dominate the artwork.” We will investigate Martin’s assertion in the context of decolonial (and) queer poetics. How might poets’ negotiations of mastery speak to the intersection of their aesthetic, linguistic, cultural, historical, and political concerns? How do poets materialize a rejection or reconfiguration of mastery within decolonial practices attentive to craft and concept? The texts we will primarily engage are African, Arab(ic), and Native American, by authors at home(s) and in diaspora. Class time will blend workshop, discussion, and writing. Assignments include a group presentation, book review, and a final portfolio.

title: MICRO-UTOPIAS
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This special topics workshop/seminar explores our capacity as poets, writers, and multi-disciplinary artists to dialogue with the world around us through a socially engaged practice. During this course we will study utopian and dystopian literature, world-making poetry, theoretical frameworks, and a variety of social philosophies and creative practices that, in the words of Carol Becker, create micro-utopias: artistic interventions that “attempt to create physical manifestations of an ideal ‘humanity’ in an inhumane world.” We will workshop and activate our creative ideas in a laboratory-like atmosphere. All art-makers are welcome. Be prepared to move, discuss, risk, play, theorize, and revise.

title: Temporal Entanglements: Writing Historical Fiction
prerequisites: Prerequisites: LITR 0210A , 1010A , 1150M , 1151Q , 1152T , 1152V , 1152Y , 1153A , 1153B , 1153C , 1153D or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: “The past is never dead, it’s not even past.” – William Faulkner How do fiction writers go about creating historical narratives? If we use Saidiya Hartman’s words as our starting point, that we live in a kind of “temporal entanglement,” where the past, present and future, “are not discrete and cut off from one another,” how might we write fiction that straddles this simultaneity? In this class, students will research and write a short work of historical fiction. We’ll discuss research methods and sources, while developing supplementary materials, such as timelines, scrapbooks, and working bibliographies, that will serve to anchor our fictional texts. Students will also workshop their research-based stories with the class. In addition, we’ll read a cross-section of historical fictions and study their various approaches. Students can expect a substantial weekly reading load on top of their individual

title: The Poetics of Mysticism
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: How does one speak the unspeakable? What rhetorical and rhythmical strategies can the poet use to write the ineffable? How can the practice of writing be used not only to document mystical experience, but to cultivate ceremonial attentiveness? This poetry workshop/seminar explores the intersection of mysticism and poetry. We will investigate how writers across traditions (Islamic mysticism, Christian mysticism, Neoplatonism, pantheism, atheistic mysticism) and eras use language to approach the divine. Through a combination of close reading, seminar-style discussion, and creative workshop, students will engage with core mystical concepts (apophasis, union, ecstasy, divine madness, love, the body, suffering, horror) and draw on these themes and strategies for inspiration into their own creative work.

title: Microfiction
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Novels unravel years, decades, or even generations, while short stories culminate in singular, brilliant moments of epiphany. Short shorts, paradoxically, are heavily theme-based and thus tend to cover eternity. In this course, we will discuss the thematic underpinnings of micro fiction, and we will visit with the ways in which short shorts are structured differently from longer stories. We will examine the idea of pacing, as it is rendered in micro fiction, so that we might learn how to use such economy of words in our own micro fiction or traditional-length fiction. Each student will turn in at least three pieces of micro fiction or magical realism, and as final projects, you will revise two of these.

title: Writers on Writing
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Offers students an introduction to the study of literature (including works from more than one genre) with special attention given to a writer's way of reading. This course will include visits to the course by contemporary writers, who will read to the class and talk about their work. Enrollment is limited to 30 students.

title: Asian American Avant-Garde (ENGL 1762X)
description: Interested students must register for ENGL 1762X.

title: Form and Theory of Fiction
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students in the following programs: Comp Lit-Engl and One For Lit (AB) Comp Lit-Engl and Two For Lit (AB) Comp Lit-Literary Translation (AB) Comparative Literature (AB) English (AB) Lit. & Cultures in English (AB) Literary Arts (AB) Literary Arts (MFA) Modern Culture and Media (AB) Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: "Form and Theory of Fiction" offers an exploration of narrative theories directed particularly at creative writers, in conjunction with a hands-on examination of contemporary fictional narrative practices. Theoretical readings include historical essays on fiction and work by Gaston Bachelard, Mieke Bal, Gilles Deleuze, and others. Enrollment limited to 20.

title: Writing: Material Differences
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: An exploration of practices that make a material difference to writing, that may change what writing is in specific cultural circumstance and locations. We will look for such differences through transcultural and translingual experiments with writing, beginning "West" and moving "East." We will engage with a selection of widely divergent writers and genres, with emphases on poetics - particularly a translated rendition of Chinese poetics (such as was taken up by Pound and became influential in English literature) - and on theories that we can use for our practice, from: Fenollosa, Foucault, Derrida, and others. Enrollment limited to 20.

title: Structuring (and De-Structuring) Novels: Special Topics Literature Seminar
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students in the following programs: Comparative Literature (AB) Comparative Literature (AM) Comparative Literature (PHD) English (AB) English (AM) English (PHD) Literary Arts (AB) Literary Arts (MFA) Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: How to structure a novel? This is a question most novelists approach with dread, because, a) all the good plots and structures have been used up; b) plots can feel annoying anyway, like a capitulation to cinema or theater; and c) nevertheless, it is impossible to write in total darkness. We’ll dispel this darkness by reading works by a range of novelists. How do these authors strike a balance between complex thought and elegant/unusual structure? And how can we, as writers, maintain narrative coherence over the course of hundreds of pages?

title: Independent Study in Reading, Research, and Writing About Literature
description: Provides advanced students with an opportunity to pursue tutorial instruction oriented toward a literary research topic.

title: Independent Study in Creative Writing
description: Offers tutorial instruction oriented toward some significant work in progress by the student. Typically taken by honors or capstone candidates in the antepenultimate or penultimate semester. See instructor to seek permission during the semester before undertaking the course of study. One advanced-level workshop is prerequisite. S/NC.

title: Honors Independent Study in Creative Writing
description: Provides tutorial instruction for students completing their theses or capstone projects. Typically taken by honors or capstone candidates in their final semester. See instructor to seek permission during the semester before undertaking the course of study. S/NC.

title: Graduate Fiction
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students in the following programs: Creative Writing (MFA) Literary Arts (MFA) Literary Arts-Electronic Wrtg (MFA) Literary Arts-Fiction (MFA) Literary Arts-Playwriting (MFA) Literary Arts-Poetry (MFA) Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Advanced practice of the art: a writing seminar, limited to graduate students in Literary Arts. Emphasis is placed on developing a better understanding of the creative process, strategies and forms. Written permission required. S/NC.

title: Graduate Poetry
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students in the following programs: Creative Writing (MFA) Literary Arts (MFA) Literary Arts-Electronic Wrtg (MFA) Literary Arts-Fiction (MFA) Literary Arts-Playwriting (MFA) Literary Arts-Poetry (MFA) Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Advanced practice of the art: a writing seminar, limited to graduate students in Literary Arts. Emphasis is placed on developing a better understanding of the creative process, strategies and forms. Written permission required. S/NC.

title: Dialogue, Monologue, & Dialect
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students in the Literary Arts (MFA) program. Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This hybrid workshop/seminar for graduate students will use works of fiction, cinema, theater, and narrative poetry as beginning points for experiments and discussions centered on a wide range of concepts and practices designed to widen how they hear and see the possibilities of voice and body language in their work. Special attention will be paid to regional and international, especially hybrid, forms of English.

title: Graduate Independent Study in Reading, Research, and Writing About Literature
description: Provides graduate students with an opportunity to pursue tutorial instruction oriented toward a literary research topic.

title: Graduate Independent Studies in Literary Writing
description: Offers tutorial instruction oriented toward some significant work in progress by the graduate student. S/NC.

title: Graduate Thesis Independent Study in Literary Writing
description: Provides tutorial instruction for graduate students completing their graduate creative theses. Typically taken in the final semester. See instructor to seek permission during the semester before undertaking the course of study. S/NC.

title: Pedagogy Seminar
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students in the Literary Arts (MFA) program. Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The Pedagogy Seminar examines ideas about teaching in a literary arts/creative writing environment. The pros and cons of the “workshop”-style will be discussed alongside alternative models, and general topics of exploration will include: creative process pedagogy, writing-to-learn, multi-genre approaches, uses of readings/research, and general classroom management. Designing an inclusive classroom and syllabus as well as exploring generative and innovative practices will be covered as well. A special emphasis will be on preparing students to feel confident and to explore a range of creative process issues. Personal writing as well as syllabus design will be expected.

title: Literary Arts Pedagogy in Practice
prerequisites: Prerequisite: LITR 2700 . Enrollment limited to students in the Literary Arts (MFA) program. Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The Pedagogy in Practice Seminar examines ideas about teaching in a literary arts/creative writing environment. The pros and cons of the “workshop”-style will be discussed alongside alternative models, and general topics of exploration will include: creative process pedagogy, writing-to-learn, multi-genre approaches, uses of readings/research, and general classroom management. This is a hands-on forum to provide guidance on how to build an inclusive, pedagogically effective meeting space. A special emphasis will be on preparing instructors to feel confident and explore a range of creative process issues. There will be opportunities to develop personal writing, especially in response to student work.

title: Graduate Independent Study in Professional Development
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students in the Literary Arts (MFA) program. Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students.
description: This half-credit course is for a Literary Arts graduate student not enrolled in a pedagogy seminar. Through this independent study, the graduate student will work with a faculty advisor to prepare for the post-MFA experience. This may include a focus on aspects of teaching, but may also focus on related professional pursuits, such as working in literary agency, publishing and professional writing.

title: Analytic Geometry and Calculus
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: MATH 0050 and 0060 provide a slower-paced introduction to calculus for students who require additional preparation. Presents the same calculus topics as MATH 0090, together with a review of the necessary precalculus topics. Students successfully completing this sequence are prepared for MATH 0100. S/NC only.

title: Analytic Geometry and Calculus
prerequisites: Prerequisite: MATH 0050 . Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: A slower-paced introduction to calculus for students who require additional preparation. Presents the same calculus topics as MATH 0090, together with a review of the necessary precalculus topics. Students successfully completing this sequence are prepared for MATH 0100. Prerequisite: MATH 0050 or written permission. May not be taken for credit in addition to MATH 0070 or MATH 0090. S/NC only.

title: Math Teaching Fellows Program
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The Math Teaching Fellows Program is a semester-long certificate program that provides UTAs with the skills and knowledge required to be an effective UTA in the Math Department. Admission into the program is by application only; participants will hold a UTA appointment in a Mathematics course approved by the Teaching Fellows Coordinators and must therefore be eligible for student employment at Brown during the term. Participants should have completed at least one of Math 0100, 0180, 0190, 0200, 0350, 0520, 0540.

title: Single Variable Calculus, Part I
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: An intensive course in calculus of one variable including limits, differentiation, maxima and minima, the chain rule, rational functions, trigonometric functions, and exponential functions. Introduction to integration with applications to area and volumes of revolution. MATH 0090 and MATH 0100 or the equivalent are recommended for all students intending to concentrate in the sciences or mathematics. May not be taken for credit in addition to MATH 0060 or MATH 0070. S/NC only.

title: Single Variable Calculus, Part II
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students.
description: A continuation of the material of MATH 90 including further development of techniques of integration. Other topics covered are infinite series, power series, Taylor's formula, polar coordinates, parametric equations, introduction to differential equations, and numerical methods. MATH 90 and 100 or the equivalent are recommended for all students intending to concentrate in mathematics or the sciences. MATH 100 may not be taken in addition to MATH 170 or MATH 190.

title: Multivariable Calculus
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Three-dimensional analytic geometry. Differential and integral calculus of functions of two or three variables: partial derivatives, multiple integrals, Green's Theorem, Stokes' theorem, and the divergence theorem. Prerequisite: MATH 100, MATH 170, or MATH 190, or advanced placement or written permission. [MATH 180 may not be taken in addition to MATH 200 or MATH 350.]

title: Single Variable Calculus, Part II (Physics/Engineering)
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course, which covers roughly the same material and has the same prerequisites as MATH 100, is intended for students with a special interest in physics or engineering. The main topics are: applications and techniques of integration; sequences, series, and power series methods; parametric equations and polar coordinates; additional topics at instructor's discretion. MATH 190 may not be taken in addition to MATH 100 or MATH 170.

title: Multivariable Calculus (Physics/Engineering)
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course, which covers roughly the same material as MATH 180, is intended for students with a special interest in physics or engineering. The main topics are: geometry of three-dimensional space; partial derivatives; Lagrange multipliers; double, surface, and triple integrals; vector analysis; Stokes' theorem and the divergence theorem, with applications to electrostatics and fluid flow. Prerequisite: MATH 100, MATH 170, or MATH 190, or advanced placement or written permission . [MATH 200 may not be taken in addition to MATH 180 or MATH 350.]

title: Multivariable Calculus With Theory
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course provides a rigorous treatment of multivariable calculus. Topics covered include vector analysis, partial differentiation, multiple integration, line integrals, Green's theorem, Stokes' theorem, and the divergence theorem. MATH 0350 covers the same material as MATH 0180, but with more emphasis on theory and on understanding proofs. Prerequisites: MATH 0100, MATH 0170, or MATH 0190, or advanced placement or written permission. [MATH 0350 may not be taken in addition to MATH 0180 or MATH 0200.]

title: Introduction to Number Theory
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: An overview of one of the most beautiful areas of mathematics. Ideal for any student who wants a taste of mathematics outside of, or in addition to, the calculus sequence. Topics include: prime numbers, congruences, quadratic reciprocity, sums of squares, Diophantine equations, and, as time permits, such topics as cryptography and continued fractions. No prerequisites.

title: Linear Algebra
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: A first course in linear algebra designed to develop students' problem solving skills, mathematical writing skills, and gain facility with the applications and theory of linear algebra. Topics will include: vector spaces, linear transformations, matrices, systems of linear equations, bases, eigenvalues, and additional topics at instructor's discretion. Applications may include differential equations, difference equations, least squares approximations, and models in economics and in biological and physical sciences. MATH 0520 or MATH 0540 is a prerequisite for all 1000-level courses in Mathematics except MATH 1260 or MATH 1610. Recommended prerequisite: MATH 0100 or equivalent. May not be taken in addition to MATH 0540.

title: Linear Algebra With Theory
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course provides a rigorous introduction to the theory of linear algebra. Topics covered include: matrices, linear equations, determinants, and eigenvalues; vector spaces and linear transformations; inner products; Hermitian, orthogonal, and unitary matrices; and Jordan normal form. MATH 540 provides a more theoretical treatment of the topics in MATH 520, and students will have opportunities during the course to develop proof-writing skills. Recommended prerequisite: MATH 100, MATH 170, or MATH 190. [MATH 540 may not be taken in addition to MATH 520.]

title: Introduction to Higher Mathematics
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This semester-long class will expose students to several fundamental areas of mathematics. It will be team taught by three members of the faculty. Topics, which will vary from year to year, will be chosen from logic and set theory, number theory, abstract algebra, combinatorics and graph theory, analysis, and geometry. Approximately 4 weeks will be devoted to each of the selected topics.

title: The Art of Writing Mathematics
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Math 1001 is an introduction to proof-writing designed to prepare students for further exploration of rigorous mathematics. Students will be trained to identify and employ a variety of proof-techniques such as direct proof, proof by contradiction, proof by induction, and proof by cases, to name a few. Mathematical topics covered include samplings of set theory, logic, and number theory, with additional topics chosen at the instructors discretion if time permits. Recommended Prerequisites: MATH 0520, MATH 0540, MATH 0100, MATH 0190, MATH 0180, MATH 0200, or MATH 0350. Students with little proof writing experience are encouraged to take MATH 1001 prior to taking, or concurrently with, other 1000-level Mathematics courses.

title: Analysis: Functions of One Variable
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0520 * , 0540 * or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. * May be taken concurrently. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Completeness properties of the real number system, topology of the real line. Proof of basic theorems in calculus, infinite series. Topics selected from ordinary differential equations. Fourier series, Gamma functions, and the topology of Euclidean plane and 3-space. Prerequisite: MATH 0180, 0200, or 0350. MATH 0520 or 0540 may be taken concurrently. Most students are advised to take MATH 1010 before MATH 1630.

title: Graph Theory
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0180 , 0200 or 0350 and ( MATH 0520 or 0540 ) or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course covers important material about graph theory, such as spanning trees, network flow problems, matching problems, coloring problems, planarity, Cayley graphs, spectral theory on graphs, and Ramsey Theory. The emphasis will be on a combination of theory and algorithms. Depending on the instructor, connections to such fields as combinatorics, geometry, or computer science might be emphasized. Prerequisite: MATH 0180, 0200 or 0350 and MATH 0520 or 0540 are recommended.

title: Fundamental Problems of Geometry
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0520 , 0540 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This class discusses geometry from a modern perspective. Topics include hyperbolic, projective, conformal, and affine geometry, and various theorems and structures built out of them. Prerequisite: MA 0520, MA 0540, or permission of the instructor.

title: Differential Geometry
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0520 , 0540 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The study of curves and surfaces in 2- and 3-dimensional Euclidean space using the techniques of differential and integral calculus and linear algebra. Topics include curvature and torsion of curves, Frenet-Serret frames, global properties of closed curves, intrinsic and extrinsic properties of surfaces, Gaussian curvature and mean curvature, geodesics, minimal surfaces, and the Gauss-Bonnet theorem.

title: Cryptography
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0520 , 0540 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course focuses on the mathematics underlying public key cryptosystems, digital signatures, and other topics in cryptography. A sampling of mathematical topics, such as groups, rings, fields, number theory, probability, complexity theory, elliptic curves, and lattices, will be introduced and applied to cryptography. No prior knowledge of these topics is assumed, nor is prior programming experience needed; any programming knowledge required will be covered in class.

title: Ordinary Differential Equations
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0520 , 0540 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Ordinary differential equations, including existence and uniqueness theorems and the theory of linear systems. Topics may also include stability theory, the study of singularities, and boundary value problems.

title: Probability
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0180 , 0200 , 0350 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Basic probability theory including random variables, distribution functions, independence, expectation, variance, and conditional expectation. Classical examples of probability density and mass functions (binomial, geometric, normal, exponential) and their applications. Stochastic processes including discrete and continuous time Poisson processes, Markov chains, and Brownian motion.

title: Mathematical Statistics
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 1210 , 1610 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Frequentist and Bayesian viewpoints and decision theory principles. Concepts from probability, including the central limit theorem and multivariate normal distributions, and asymptotic estimates. Inferences from independent, identically distributed sampling: point estimation, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) and generalized linear models of regression.

title: Topics in Functional Analysis
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0520 , 0540 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Infinite-dimensional vector spaces with applications to some or all of the following topics: Fourier series and integrals, distributions, differential equations, integral equations, calculus of variations. Prerequisite: At least one 1000-level course in Mathematics or Applied Mathematics, or permission of the instructor.

title: Complex Analysis
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0180 , 0200 , 0350 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Examines one of the cornerstones of mathematics. Complex differentiability, Cauchy-Riemann differential equations, contour integration, residue calculus, harmonic functions, geometric properties of complex mappings. Prerequisite: MATH 0180, 0200, or 0350. This course does not require MATH 0520 or 0540.

title: Abstract Algebra
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0520 , 0540 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: A proof-based course that introduces the principles and concepts of modern abstract algebra. Topics will include groups, rings, and fields, with applications to number theory, the theory of equations, and geometry. Previous proof-writing experience is not required. MATH 1530 is required of all students concentrating in mathematics. It is strongly suggested that potential mathematics concentrators make MATH 1530 one of the first thousand level mathematics classes that they take.

title: Topics in Abstract Algebra
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 1530 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Galois theory together with selected topics in algebra. Examples of subjects which have been presented in the past include algebraic curves, group representations, and the advanced theory of equations. Prerequisite: MATH 1530.

title: Number Theory
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 1530 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Selected topics in number theory will be investigated. Unique factorization, prime numbers, modular arithmetic, arithmetic functions, quadratic reciprocity, finite fields, and related topics. Prerequisite: MATH 1530 or written permission.

title: Real Analysis I
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 0520 , 0540 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: A rigorous introduction to real analysis, this course treats topics in point set topology, function spaces, differentiability of functions on Euclidean spaces, and Fourier series. Among the many topics and theorems we investigate in detail will be connectedness and compactness, the Arzela-Ascoli theorem, the inverse and implicit function theorems, and L^2 and pointwise convergence of Fourier series. It is recommended that a student take MATH 1010 before attempting MATH 1630.

title: Real Analysis II
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 1630 , 1130 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: A second course in real analysis, in this class we study measure theory and integration as well as Hilbert spaces. Among the many topics we study will be abstract measure and integration theory, Fourier transform, linear functionals and the Riesz representation theorem, compact operators, and the spectral theorem. The course may also include additional material of interest to the students and instructor.

title: Topology
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 1530 and ( MATH 1010 , 1630 or 1130 ) or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Topology of Euclidean spaces, winding number and applications, knot theory, fundamental group and covering spaces. Eulercharacteristic, simplicial complexes, classification of two-dimensional manifolds, vector fields, the Poincaré-Hopf theorem, andintroduction to three-dimensional topology. Prerequisites: Abstract Algebra (Math 1530) and Real Analysis (Math 1010 or Math 1630).

title: Manifolds
prerequisites: Prerequisites: ( MATH 0520 or 0540 ) and MATH 1530 and 1010 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Manifolds are a basic concept that unite ideas from linear algebra, multivariable calculus, geometry, and topology. Math and physics research require a familiarity with manifolds. Topics covered include the definition of a smooth manifold either as a smooth subset of R^n or as an abstract manifold, coordinating charts, and transition functions. The course will cover concrete examples: e.g. surfaces and 3-manifolds.Other topics include tangent vectors, vector fields, tensors, exterior algebra, differential forms, change of coordinates, Stokes’ Theorem and the basics of De Rham cohomology.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Euclidean Geometry
prerequisites: Prerequisites: ( MATH 0520 or 0540 ) and ( MATH 1530 or 1540 ) or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course explores the exciting field of automated reasoning systems for complex mathematical problems, inspired by Google DeepMind’s AlphaGeometry. We’ll delve into the mathematical foundations of such systems, starting with the essentials of Euclidean geometry from Euclid’s Elements, including axioms and deductive reasoning. The course structure combines theoretical learning with hands-on experience. We will also focus on the practical application of building a Euclidean geometry reasoning system. Students will work in groups throughout the semester to develop their own system to solve geometry problems. **Admission into this class is by application only. Please contact the Instructors for more information**

title: Introduction to Lie Algebras
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 1530 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Lie groups and Lie algebras are important, because they are the symmetries of structures such as quadratic forms, differential systems and smooth manifolds. The prototype of a Lie algebra is the space of 3-vectors together with their cross product, which is closely related to the Lie group of rotations. We will see how this basic example generalizes, mostly in the context of matrices. We'll examine special types of Lie algebras, such as nilpotent, solvable and semi-simple, study root systems and their diagrams, explore some representation theory, and end with the classification of the simple Lie algebras. Prerequisite: MATH 1530.

title: Honors Conference
description: Collateral reading, individual conferences. Section numbers vary by instructor. Please check Banner for the correct section number and CRN to use when registering for this course.

title: Differential Geometry
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Introduction to differential geometry (differentiable manifolds, differential forms, tensor fields, homogeneous spaces, fiber bundles, connections, and Riemannian geometry), followed by selected topics in the field. Pre-reqs: MATH 2110 and undergraduates require permission from the instructor.

title: Algebraic Geometry
prerequisites: Prerequisite: MATH 2510 * . * May be taken concurrently. Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Complex manifolds and algebraic varieties, sheaves and cohomology, vector bundles, Hodge theory, Kähler manifolds, vanishing theorems, the Kodaira embedding theorem, the Riemann-Roch theorem, and introduction to deformation theory. Pre-reqs: MATH 2510 and MATH 2520 and undergraduates require permission from the instructor.

title: Algebraic Geometry
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: See Algebraic Geometry (MATH 2050) for course description.

title: Introduction to Manifolds
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Inverse Function Theorem, manifolds and submanifolds, tangent and cotangent bundles, transversality, flows and vector fields, Frobenius Theorem, vector bundles, tensors and differential forms, Sard’s Theorem, introduction to Lie groups. Pre-reqs: MATH 1060, MATH 1140 and preferably MATH 1410 and undergraduates require permission from the instructor.

title: Real Function Theory
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Real numbers, outer measures, measures, Lebesgue measure, integrals of measurable functions, Holder and Minkowski inequalities, modes of onvergence, L^p spaces, product measures, Fubini's Theorem, signed measures, Radon-Nikodym theorem, dual space of L^p and of C, Hausdorff measure.

title: Real Function Theory
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The basics of Hilbert space theory, including orthogonal projections, the Riesz representation theorem, and compact operators. The basics of Banach space theory, including the open mapping theorem, closed graph theorem, uniform boundedness principle, Hahn-Banach theorem, Riesz representation theorem (pertaining to the dual of C_0(X)), weak and weak-star topologies. Various additional topics, possibly including Fourier series, Fourier transform, ergodic theorems, distribution theory, and the spectral theory of linear operators.

title: Complex Function Theory
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Introduction to the theory of analytic functions of one complex variable. Content varies somewhat from year to year, but always includes the study of power series, complex line integrals, analytic continuation, conformal mapping, and an introduction to Riemann surfaces.

title: Complex Function Theory
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: See Complex Function Theory (MATH 2250) for course description.

title: Partial Differential Equations
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MATH 1610 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The theory of the classical partial differential equations; the method of characteristics and general first order theory. The Fourier transform, the theory of distributions, Sobolev spaces, and techniques of harmonic and functional analysis. More general linear and nonlinear elliptic, hyperbolic, and parabolic equations and properties of their solutions, with examples drawn from physics, differential geometry, and the applied sciences. Semester II of this course concentrates on special topics chosen by the instructor.

title: Topology
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: An introduction to algebraic topology. Topics include fundamental group, covering spaces, simplicial and singular homology, CW complexes, and an introduction to cohomology. Pre-reqs: MATH 1410, MATH 1530, and MATH 1010 and/or MATH 1130 and undergraduates require permission from the instructor.

title: Algebraic Topology
prerequisites: Prerequisite: MATH 2410 . Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This is a continuation of MATH 2410. Topics include cohomology, cup products, Poincare duality, and other topics chosen by the instructor.

title: Algebra
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Basic properties of groups, rings, fields, and modules. Topics include: finite groups, representations of groups, rings with minimum condition, Galois theory, local rings, algebraic number theory, classical ideal theory, basic homological algebra, and elementary algebraic geometry. Pre-reqs: MATH 1530 and MATH 1540 and undergraduates require permission from the instructor.

title: Algebra
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: See Algebra (MATH 2510) for course description. Pre-reqs: MATH 2510 and undergraduates require permission from the instructor.

title: Number Theory
prerequisites: Prerequisite: MATH 2510 * . * May be taken concurrently. Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Introduction to algebraic and analytic number theory. Topics covered during the first semester include number fields, rings of integers, primes and ramification theory, completions, adeles and ideles, and zeta functions. Content of the second semester varies from year to year; possible topics include class field theory, arithmetic geometry, analytic number theory, and arithmetic K-theory. Pre-reqs: MATH 2510 and undergraduates require permission from the instructor.

title: Number Theory
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: See Number Theory (MATH 2530) for course description.

title: Advanced Topics in Mathematics
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.

title: Arithmetic dynamics: current trends and future directions
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Graduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course will be an introduction to arithmetic dynamics, a relatively new area which studies dynamical systems over number fields and other fields of arithmetic interest. We will focus especially on the interplay between the local geometry of a dynamical system and the arithmetic of its forward orbits. The class will develop the necessary complex dynamics and non-archimedean geometry in dimension 1, and will also explore connections/applications to higher-dimensional dynamical systems and abelian varieties, along with some open problems. The target audience covers a wide range of interests in the areas of number theory, algebraic geometry, and dynamics.

title: Preliminary Exam Preparation
description: No description available.

title: Reading and Research
description: Independent research or course of study under the direction of a member of the faculty, which may include research for and preparation of a thesis. Section numbers vary by instructor. Please check Banner for the correct section number and CRN to use when registering for this course.

title: Thesis Preparation
description: For graduate students who have met the residency requirement and are continuing research on a full time basis.

title: Courses of Interest to Students Majoring in Mathematics
Text/Media/Culture: Theories of Modern Culture and Media
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This introductory course will explore its three key terms "modern," "culture," and "media" through a variety of theories, historical narratives, and media objects. We will ask how different media—such as print, photography, cinema, television, digital art, online video, archival practices, and social media—yield distinct modes of seeing, thinking, and feeling, structure the ways we act and engage with the common world, and communicate and collaborate. We will read semiotic theory, critical race studies, feminist, post-colonial, queer and political theory, and examine concepts such as textuality, visuality, and networks. Open to undergraduates only.

title: Television Studies
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Introduces students to the rigorous study of television, concentrating on televisual formations (texts, industry, audience) in relation to social/cultural formations (gender, generational, and family dynamics; constructions of race, class, and nation; consumerism and global economic flows). That is, this course considers both how television has been defined and how television itself defines the terms of our world. Students MUST register for the lecture section, the screening, and a conference section. Open to undergraduates only.

title: Cinematic Coding and Narrativity
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Introduces students to rigorous study of the structural and ideological attributes of cinema, concentrating on the dominant narrative model developed in the American studio system and alternatives to that model. Attention to film theory in relation to questions of representation, culture, and society. Students become conversant with specific elements and operations of the cinematic apparatus (e. g. camerawork, editing, sound-image relations) and how they produce discursive meanings. Open to undergraduates only.

title: Introduction to the Production Image
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The course will provide students with a basic introduction to digital sound and image acquisition and post-production, and to consider the particular capabilities of these digital technologies, especially as these relate to the production of meaning. Of particular interest will be the representational limits of these technologies at the intersection of science and art. Classes will be organized as workshop environments where extensive class time will be devoted to hands-on learning with digital film cameras, lighting, and digital sound recorders. There are no prerequisites for this class.

title: Introduction to DJ Culture
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Drawing on histories and subcultures of electronic dance music, alongside theories of sampling, mixing, remixing and postproduction, this practice-based course offers a basic introduction to DJ and production technologies as well as an exploration of world making tactics via the manipulation of sound and space. How do DJs manipulate sound to stitch together a unique sonic narrative? How is DJing itself a kind of theorizing through sound? Topics include the history of the DJ from the early 20th century to the rise of DJ-focused dance musics, from hip-hop to techno. Students will have hands-on experience with CDJs and throughout the semester, students will work on a series of short sound exercises and a final or collaborative project. There are no prerequisites for this class.

title: Introduction to Filmic Practice: Time and Form
prerequisites: Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: A studio-style course on working with time based media, focused specifically on the technology of 16mm film production. With its focus on photographic and montage processes, as well as lighting and sound, the principles established in this course provide a solid foundation for all subsequent work in media, whether cinematic, video or new media, and it is strongly advised as a foundation level, skills oriented media course. Students produce a series of short, non-sync films. No previous experience required. Screenings, demonstrations and studio work.

title: Transnational Cinemas in East Asia
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 01 or 02. Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This first-year seminar comparatively studies the forms and histories of East Asian cinemas in the context of transnationalism and globalization. As the notion of nation-state has increasingly been problematized, the concept of “national cinema,” which frames cinematic works according to their countries of origin, has come into question. This course revisits this theoretical debate by exploring the transnational connections among different film cultures in China, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Japan, and South Korea. The course will emphasize as much the local diversities and specificities as the stylistic, generic, thematic, and industrial conjunctions across national/cultural boundaries. While surveying major genres and auteurs in East Asian cinemas, such as Wong Kar-Wai, Hou Hsiao-Hsien, Ozu, Kurosawa, Park Chan-wook and Bong Joon-ho, through the theoretical frameworks of nationalism, transnationalism, post-colonialism, and globalism, the course will particularly focus on the shifting representation of identities of gender,

title: DIY: Notes from the Underground
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 01 or 02. Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: DIY: Notes from the Underground explores DIY and alternative creative practices, communities and subcultures through the potentiality of the “underground” as a site of radical making. But what, exactly, do we mean by “underground”? Readings, discussions and screenings will engage theories of situationism, hacking, the remix, subculture and opacity alongside histories of DIY creative communities, punk, zines, fashion, and the garage as a site of creativity. Students will engage with “underground” subcultural productions across music, contemporary art and performance, fashion, film and television. Overall, this course is an exploration of DIY as a critique of cultural production under capitalism, with an emphasis on experimentation and creativity “underground.

title: Reading Practices: An Introduction to Literary Theory (ENGL 0800R)
description: Interested students must register for ENGL 0800R.

title: Take Me Apart: The Body As Myth Between Literature, Film, and Theory
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MCM 0100 , 0110 , 0150 , 0220 , 0230 , 0240 , 0250 , 0260 or 1110 . Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Through various media such as novels, films, and performance art, together we will interrogate our primary question: what is a body? ‘The Body As Myth’ explores the work of artists and scholars across disciplinary fields that have discussed and dissected the concept of the body as an amalgamation of biological, sociocultural, psychological, and/or philosophical schemas rather than the pure instrument of Cartesian dualism. Topics that we will encounter over the course of the semester include horror and the tortured body, phenomenology, the libidinal self, race and the body, feeling and cinema, and more. This course has required asynchronous film screenings each week.

title: The Dancer and The Muse
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MCM 0100 , 0110 , 0150 , 0220 , 0230 , 0240 , 0250 , 0260 or 1110 . Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: It has by now become a cliché to assert––rightfully so––that aestheticians and philosophers have neglected dance by reducing the female dancer to a muse. In this course, rather than insisting on this fact, we will unpack the relation between the dancer and her proximity to the concept of a muse. We will read canonical and contemporary texts on the philosophy of dance, and watch dance performances and films, to reinterpret this provocative, elusive concept and follow the unconventional path it opens up for the criticism and interpretation of contemporary dance and cinema. Students who have not fulfilled the prerequisite may apply for permission to enroll by requesting an override in Courses@Brown.

title: Between Media and Ecology: Life, Technology, and Inhabitation
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MCM 0100 , 0110 , 0150 , 0220 , 0230 , 0240 , 0250 , 0260 or 1110 . Enrollment is limited to Undergraduate level students. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course examines the entwinement between media and ecology, exploring how they affect, structure, and shape living systems. We understand “ecology” not just as a thematic concern of media content or as a scientific and cultural narrative on media platforms. Instead, we approach it as a critical framework to rethink perception, relationality, embodiment, and systems of meaning. We analyze the material conditions—geophysical resources, labor, and infrastructures—that enable media technologies and explore “media” as a dynamic process of “mediation” and networks of technologies of power. In an era of climate crises, we ask how ecological and media systems coconstitute subjectivity, community, and political agency. Our texts and media objects will prompt us to challenge the very concepts of “media” and “ecology,” as we think through our situated, material conditions of being and relationality.

title: Experiments in NonFiction Film and Media
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: We live in a time of unprecedented access to both making and consuming media. Holding a creative practice offers a space to slow down, observe, and reflect. This course introduces students to creative practice as a way of seeing, sensing, and reflecting on the world. While work across mediums is welcome, we will focus on filmmaking not only as a technical skill, but as a mode of inquiry — a process of listening, experimenting, and developing one’s creative voice. Students will engage in hands-on experimentation exploring nonfiction shapes experience, memory, and perception while drawing from critical theory to question binaries of theory and practice. By the end of the semester, students will complete several short experiments and one final nonfiction project that synthesizes both theoretical and personal insight into their own creative practice.

title: What We Talk About When We Talk About Horror
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 03, 04, 05, 06, 07, 08, 09, 10, 11, 12 or 13. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The killer, their victims, the ‘terrible place,’ the ‘final girl’: components like these, associated with 1970s American slasher films, tend to dominate popular accounts of the genre—but the cinema of fear and sensation is a dynamic mode of contemporary filmmaking, transnational in its origins and circulation, and thematically and formally eclectic. This course will study the horror genre through scholarly and critical debates, emphasizing the ways both experts and enthusiasts have theorized horror's significance and social urgency, the unique properties of horror spectatorship, and the formal attributes of individual films. Each week will focus on a specific conceptual area, including monstrosity, the uncanny, Otherness, excess, allegory, haunting, and abjection.

title: Let’s Get Physical: Histories and Theories of Fitness and Wellness
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Did you know that the treadmill originated as a prison torture device? This course will examine the body and technology, as well as exercise and wellness cultures across media. Through readings in critical theory, media studies, gender studies, disability studies, and science and technology studies, students will explore the spaces and instruments of fitness, as well as the cultures that have emerged around them. Looking at a variety of texts, including treadmill patents and live-streamed Peloton classes, we will ask what the history of exercise tells us about gender, perceptions of health and disability, and our relationship to technology. Utilizing different research methods, including interviewing an Instagram fitness influencer and conducting autoethnography at the Nelson Gym, we will interrogate theories of “fitness,” from eugenics up through contemporary wellness fads such as biohacking, polygenic embryo screening, and “Make America Healthy Again.”

title: Iranian Cinema
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MCM 0100 , 0110 , 0150 , 0220 , 0230 , 0240 , 0250 , 0260 , 1110 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 05, 06, 07, 08, 09, 10, 11, 12 or 13. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The emergence in the 1990s of Iranian cinema onto the world stage caught many by surprise. This cinema has, however, had a long and illustrious history. While attempting to provide an historical survey of these films, we will focus primarily on those produced in the last two decades. We will pay close attention to cinematic form but will also examine the ways the films intersect with cultural-political events, including the Revolution and the subsequent Islamicization of the culture, the institutionalization of the “modesty system,” and the alteration of divorce laws.

title: Kiarostami: What is an Image?
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MCM 0100 , 0110 , 0150 , 0220 , 0230 , 0240 , 0250 , 0260 , 1110 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 05, 06, 07, 08, 09, 10, 11, 12 or 13. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: In the history of cinema, a small number of directors have been treated as if their work stood for cinema itself. Abbas Kiarostami is one of these. Yet while his films foreground the act of cinema, they also address questions of reality to which the “neorealist” label cannot respond. To existing attempts to analyze his films in this light, we will add a principia domestica. Our question is this: how is it that the mystical, Islamic background from which Kiarostami invents is able to respond to current debates about reality in compelling, modern terms?

title: Virtual Bodies: Play, Power, Performance (AMST 1906V)
description: Interested students must register for AMST 1906V.

title: Practices of Autofiction (ENGL 1901T)
description: Interested students must register for ENGL 1901T.

title: In the Cut
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MCM 0100 , 0110 , 0150 , 0220 , 0230 , 0240 , 0250 , 0260 , 1110 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Enrollment limited to students with a semester level of 05, 06, 07, 08, 09, 10, 11, 12 or 13. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: The technique of film editing has been a source of enduring fascination for theorists, propagandists, digital cinephiles, and avant-garde experimenters alike. This film studies seminar aims to deep dive below the line into editing in terms of theory and practice, and art and labor. We will read theoretical accounts of editing techniques and effects; we will mine practitioners’ craft discourse for conceptual insights; and we will comparatively analyze pairs of films for formal traces of editing’s function and potency. This course is openly suited to theory concentrators interested in historical accounts of how film editing works (both within films and on spectators), and practice concentrators interested in historicizing and contextualizing the compositional choices they make and consume. Students of both persuasions will have an opportunity to develop creative as well as critical projects.

title: Animatedness: History and Theory of Animation
prerequisites: Prerequisites: MCM 0150 , 0220 , 0230 , 0240 , 0250 , 0260 , 1110 or minimum score of WAIVE in 'Graduate Student PreReq'. Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course explores the history and theory of animation through the notion of “animatedness,” a concept raised by Sianne Ngai to describe a racialized feeling of being moved both physically and emotionally, an affect that is deeply rooted in the forms and techniques of animation. We will examine animation’s historical development and stylistic possibilities, from the earliest stop-motion animations to the latest computer graphic techniques, exploring the aesthetics of animation as a fertile ground to interrogate the relationship between changing technological conditions and often-racialized bodily experience in modern society marked by mobility, fragmentation, and demarcation. It is also meant to give you a different way to think about the basic concepts in film history and theory, moving away from the assumption of an essentially photographic medium to consider different ways in which moving images are produced, received, and appreciated.

title: Politics and Film 3: Adaptation
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Beginning with examples of repetition, adaptation, or re-making in feminist, literary, or political theory, cinema studies and philosophy, legal studies and history, (eg., Butler, Cavell, Douglass, Morrison, Spillers) this seminar will study the re-do: looking at filmic remakes/adaptations in political/cultural contexts as iterative, citational, augmentative/aspirational or perfectionist. The aim is to consider such efforts in connection with debates in the fields of Adaptation Studies, structuralism/post structuralism, Classics' “reception studies.” The focus is on adaptation as a site of contestation. Is it conservative reclamation? or radical reform/abolition of contemporary norms, genders, institutions, and politics? Final projects can be essays or performance pieces, adaptations or analyses. All will be shared collectively in the final class.

title: Writing: Material Differences (LITR 1230J)

title: Theory for Practice / Practice as Theory
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This advanced seminar explores the tensions between theory and practice in contemporary media and art works. The course examines how recent creative practices use theoretical concepts, and how practices today often include textual production or crucial theoretical implications. Requirements include: a major production project, short papers, presentations of work-in-progress, and weekly readings and screenings. Application required. Application is available in the MCM office. Students must bring a completed application to the first class to be considered for admission. The final class list will be determined after this meeting, with permission of the instructor. Enrollment limited to 20. S/NC.

title: Experimental Narrative
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: With film well into its 2nd century, a large body of work has emerged that plays with, around, and against conventions of classical cinema. Specifically, what we understand to be traditional narrative structures, such as drama, documentary, and action films. In fact, experimental narrative now has some of its own genres, which are to be found in both mainstream and fringe media. The goal of this class is to investigate some of these forms of experimental narrative. It is predicated on a basic understanding of narrative conventions, and designed to encourage students to make work that challenges those conventions.

title: Nonbinary Thought and Elemental Media Practices
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: Engaging what Denise Ferreira da Silva has called “elemental cinema", this class pauses in the interval of the false binary between theory and practice in the arts. We will read texts, view or listen to artwork, and craft responses in multiple materialities. Readings in Black feminist thought, transmedia, tidalectics, decolonial method, poethics, and new materialisms will accompany our engagement with artworks that challenge the habits and limits of screenal, embodied, or narrative forms (Bellmore, Shonibare, Whittle, Cassils, Warhol, Jaffa, Vo, Lemon, Gumbs, Rosenbach/Nauman, Bey among others). On class days without assigned reading/viewing students will share their own creative responses, collaborative or solo, in their chosen visual, object, textual, sonic, or performance-based work.

title: Advanced Digital & Cross-Disciplinary Language Arts Workshop (LITR 1010H)
description: Interested students must register for LITR 1010H.

title: Experimental Video and Emerging Media
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This production course explores experimental video and media practices that challenge conventional cinematic forms and modes of storytelling. Each week, we will engage with the aesthetic and critical dimensions of a range of experimental approaches, including essay films, video poetry, elemental and immersive media environments, video game documentaries, and forms of micro-cinema—such as internet memes and animated GIFs—as well as critical engagements with generative AI. Through hands-on workshops in video and image-making, alongside screenings and discussions, students will complete short creative assignments and develop a major final project. Guest lectures by invited artists and filmmakers will provide insight into current moving-image practices. The course will culminate in a group screening and exhibition at the MCM-Carriage House.

title: Activating the Archive: Contemporary Film Practice
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This film and media production course explores historical and contemporary archives as sites of both artistic and critical intervention. Engaging with questions of inscription, memory, erasure, and historical re-imagination, students will examine how artists and filmmakers repurpose archival materials to craft counter-histories and speculative narratives. Through workshops in video and image-making, screenings, discussions, and guest lectures by invited artists and filmmakers, students will engage with archives as spaces for critical inquiry and poetic retrieval. Each student will select one or more archives to develop a major project that reinterprets, recontextualizes, or reimagines the retrieved material. The course will culminate in a final group exhibition and screening at the MCM Carriage House.

title: TV/Video: Theory/Practice
prerequisites: Students in the Remote Study / Study Abroad cohort may not enroll.
description: This course examines both commercial television and independent video forms, considering their connections and/or tensions and the dialogue between them. Combining theory and practice (involving readings in media studies, TV and video screenings, critical writing, and media production assignments using available technologies), this course will give students an understanding of television and independent video histories and forms, encourage students to critique commercial media, and allow them to produce alternatives through their own creative practice. In this way, students will gain a broad understanding of televisual/videographic media, from the mainstream through the marginalized, and have an opportunity to engage with and intervene in media formations and histories in both scholarly and creative ways.
